description: Course Description: Comparative analysis of class/race/gender inequality, concentrating on the ways in which beliefs about descent, "blood," and biological difference interact with property and marital systems to affect the distribution of power in society.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ANT 002 recommended.

title: Gender & Sexuality
description: Course Description: Gender and sexuality in foraging bands, horticultural and pastoral tribes, agricultural and industrial states. Debates on cultural evolution and distribution of gender hierarchies. Impact of politics, economics, religion, social practices, women's movements on gender and sexuality. Culture, nature and sexuality.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ANT 002 recommended.

title: Dispossession & Dynamism in Africa
description: Course Description: Critical issues in the anthropology of Africa using history, ethnography, and literature. Representations of Africa, wealth in people, missionization, anti-colonial resistance, post/colonialism, resource extraction, and digital technologies.

title: Cultures & Societies of East & South Africa
description: Course Description: Ethnographic survey of Eastern and Southern Africa with analyses of selected societies which illustrate problems of interest to anthropologists. Major consideration will be given to continuities and discontinuities between periods prior to European contact and the present.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ANT 002 recommended.

title: Indigenous Peoples of the Arctic
description: Course Description: Comparative history of Indigenous peoples across the circumpolar arctic. Arctic ecosystems, language, social structure, subsistence, gender, religion, art, health, colonialism, contemporary issues, climate change, and resilience.

title: People of the Arctic: Contemporary & Historic Cultures of the Circumpolar Region
description: Course Description: Social, economic, political, and religious lives of Russian, American, Canadian, and Greenlandic Arctic people (Yup'ik, Iñupiat, Inuit). Topics include Arctic ecosystems, archaeological record of human occupation, ethnohistorical and ethnographic accounts, arctic people in popular culture, and contemporary issues.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ANT 002 or ANT 003 recommended.

title: Peoples of the Middle East
description: Course Description: Peoples of the Middle East (including North Africa). Discussions of class relations, kinship organization, sex/gender systems, religious beliefs and behavior, ethnic relations, political systems. Impact of world systems, political and religious movements and social change. (Former ANT 136.)
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ANT 002 recommended.

title: Ethnology of Southeast Asia
description: Course Description: Patterns of culture and social organization from prehistory to the present, in the context of historical, ecological, economic, and political settings. Emphasis on the relation of ethnic minorities to national states.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ANT 002 recommended.

title: Contemporary Societies & Cultures of Latin America
description: Course Description: Introduction to contemporary social structure of Latin America. Origins, maintenance and changes in inequality: economic responses to poverty, sociocultural responses to discrimination, and political responses to powerlessness.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ANT 002 recommended.

title: Performance, Embodiment, & Space in South Asia
description: Course Description: South Asian cultures and societies with a focus on performance, embodiment, and space from several disciplinary fields. Topics may include colonialism, nationalism, religious traditions, media, popular culture, cities, social movements, modernity, body-cultures, identity, gender, and diasporas.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ANT 002; or consent of instructor.

title: Topics in the Anthropology of Europe
description: Course Description: Recent ethnographies of different nation-states and socio-political spaces in Europe. Topics include the question of old and new boundaries, historical and contemporary constructions of Europe, migration and ethnicity, citizenship, belonging, multiculturalism, and post/socialisms.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ANT 002 recommended.

title: Modern South Asia Cinema
description: Course Description: South Asian cinema of last 100 years in the context of cultural, social, and political changes. South Asian history, Independence, Partition, urban life, class, migration, postcolonial identity, diaspora, gender, sexuality, religion, sport, performance, etc.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Upper division standing or consent of instructor.

title: Culture & Political Economy in Contemporary China
description: Course Description: Examining contemporary Chinese culture and political economy through reading ethnographic studies on recent transformations in rural and urban Chinese society. Special attention is given to state power, popular culture, spatial mobility, city space, and gender.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ANT 002 recommended.

title: Evolution & Human Health
description: This version has ended; see updated course, below.Course Description: Potential health issues related to human anatomical and physiological features through the lens of human evolutionary history and recent changes (e.g., industrialization).
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ANT 001 or BIS 002C recommended.

title: Primate Evolution
description: Course Description: Origin and relationships of the prosimians, monkeys, and apes.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ANT 001 or BIS 002B or BIS 002C or EVE 010 recommended.

title: Human Evolution
description: Course Description: Nature and results of the evolutionary processes involved in the formation and differentiation of humankind.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ANT 001 recommended.

title: Human Genetics: Mutation & Migration
description: This version has ended; see updated course, below.Course Description: Introduction to human genetics. Principles of inheritance, the human genome, population genetics, mutation, genetic diversity, using DNA to study ancient human history, personal genomics. Human genetics as a tool to understand the patterns and processes of human migration. Introduction to the major concepts in human genetic and genomic research.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): BIS 002B C- or better or MCB 010 C- or better.

title: The Evolution of Primate Behavior
description: Course Description: Examines ecological diversity and evolution of social systems of prosimians, monkeys, and apes, placing the social behavior of the primates in the context of appropriate ecological and evolutionary theory.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ANT 001 or ANT 054 or EVE 010 recommended.

title: Primate Behavior: Methods & Experimental Design
description: Course Description: Scientific methods of studying, describing and analyzing the behavior and ecology of primates.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): (ANT 054 or ANT 154A or NPB 102); (STA 013 or STA 013Y or STA 032 or STA 100 or SOC 046B); ANT 154CL (can be concurrent).

title: Laboratory in Primate Behavior
description: This version has ended; see updated course, below.Course Description: Design and conduct of scientific "field studies" of the behavior of group-living primates at the California National Primate Research Center.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): (ANT 054 or ANT 154A or NPB 102); (STA 013 or STA 013Y or STA 032 or STA 100 or SOC 046B); ANT 154C concurrent enrollment required.

title: Primate Conservation Biology
description: Course Description: Study of the taxonomic, ecological and cultural diversity of Primates and how human activities impact tropical ecosystems. Emphasis on case studies and applied research methods. Includes discussion about career opportunities in conservation.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ANT 001 or ANT 054 recommended.

title: Human Osteology
description: Course Description: Human skeleton from archaeological, forensic, and paleontological perspectives, including anatomical nomenclature, variation with sex and age, function, evolution, growth, and development of bones and teeth. Hands-on study and identification of human skeletal remains.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ANT 001 or ANT 001Y recommended.

title: Advanced Human Osteology
description: Course Description: Human skeletons from archaeological, forensic, and paleontological contexts. Bone and tooth structure, growth, and development; measurement, statistics, and biomechanics; assessment of age, sex, weight, height, and ancestry; and indicators of illness, injuries, diet, and activities.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ANT 156A; or equivalent.

title: Advanced Human Genetics
description: Course Description: Advanced concepts in human genetics and genomics. Identification of genes underlying human health and disease. Use of genomic data in clinical settings and examination of biases associated with 'personalized medicine." Emphasis on current human genomic technology and critical reading of scientific papers.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ANT 153 or BIS 101 or BIS 101V or EVE 102 or EVE 131 or EVE 175 or MCB 162 or MCB 182.

title: Advanced Human Genetics Lab
description: Course Description: Computer lab in human genetics and genomics. Emphasizes hands-on engagement with human genetic/genomic data. Ancestry analysis, pedigrees, de novo Mendelian disease.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ANT 153 or EVE 102 or EVE 131 or EVE 175 or MCB 162 or MCB 182; concurrent enrollment in ANT 157 encouraged.

title: The Evolution of Sex: A Biological Perspective
description: Course Description: Current theoretical frameworks for explaining the evolution of sex differences and for understanding the interrelationship between biological processes and cultural construction of gender roles.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ANT 001 recommended.

title: Disease Outbreaks in Humans and Other Primates
description: Course Description: Impacts of infectious diseases on human and other primate populations, illustrated with past and present epidemiological studies from around the world. Theoretical concepts and applied questions, from local outbreaks to pandemics.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ANT 001 or ANT 001Y or BIS 002B; or consent of instructor.

title: Neandertals & Modern Human Origins
description: Course Description: Origins, evolution, and disappearance of Neandertals. Emergence of humans like us in both anatomy and behavior. Interpretation of the fossil and archaeological records of Europe and Africa. Genetics of living and fossil humans.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ANT 001 or ANT 001Y or equivalent recommended.

title: Archeological Theory & Method
description: Course Description: History of the philosophical propositions and conceptual foundations of archaeological theories from the 18th century to present, and their relationship to the research techniques, procedures, and strategies of archaeological methods.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ANT 003 recommended.

title: New World Prehistory: The First Arrivals
description: This version has ended; see updated course, below.Course Description: Survey of data relating to the peopling of the New World. Cultural adaptation and development of early inhabitants of North and South America.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ANT 003 recommended.

title: European Archaeology: First Peopling to the Bronze Age
description: Course Description: Survey of the archaeology of Europe from its earliest inhabitants, to the Neandertals and humans, and through early agricultural and complex societies. Analysis and interpretation of the record for understanding major processes and transitions.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ANT 003 recommended.

title: Andean Prehistory: Archaeology of the Incas & Their Ancestors
description: This version has ended; see updated course, below.Course Description: Prehistory of the Andean region, especially Peru, from the earliest hunting and gathering societies through the Inca. Focus on the use of archaeological data to reconstruct ancient human adaptations to the varied Andean environments.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ANT 003 recommended.

title: California Archaeology
description: Course Description: Discussion and analysis of archaeology and archaeological practice in California. Emphasis on precontact periods and earliest contact, from the late Pleistocene through to time of the Spanish Missions.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ANT 003 recommended.

title: African Archaeology: From Australopithecines to Great Zimbabwe
description: Course Description: African archaeological record from early human ancestors, through modern human origins, into early agricultural and complex societies, the Bantu expansion, and early state formation. Focus on major processes and transitions.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ANT 003 recommended.

title: Hunter-Gatherers
description: Course Description: Study and interpretation of the ancient and modern lifeway in which peoples support themselves with primitive technologies and without benefit of domesticated plants and animals.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ANT 003 recommended.

title: Asian Prehistory
description: Course Description: Survey of the prehistory of Asia from the earliest human occupations to the rise of complex societies. Special focus on fossil and archeological records.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ANT 003 recommended.

title: Zooarchaeology
description: Course Description: Theories and methods for studying animal skeletal remains from archaeological sites. Identification and quantification of zooarchaeological material, cultural and natural processes affecting animal bones pre- and postburial, and use of faunal remains for determining past human diets and past environments.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ANT 001 or ANT 003 recommended.

title: Archaeological Field Methods
description: This version has ended; see updated course, below.Course Description: Survey of archeological field methods and techniques. Strategies for survey and site location, mapping of artifacts and features, geophysical techniques, and hand excavation and analysis of stratigraphy.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ANT 003.

title: Field Course in Archeological Methods
description: Course Description: On-site course using archaeological methods and techniques held at a field location in the western United States, generally California or Nevada. Incorporates basic methods of archaeological survey, mapping, and excavation.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ANT 181; or consent of instructor.

title: Archaeometry
description: Course Description: Scientific techniques used to study the chemical and physical properties of archaeological materials. Types of anthropological questions that can be addressed with different methods. Preparation and analysis of archaeological materials.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ANT 003 recommended.

title: Laboratory in Archeological Analysis
description: Course Description: Museum preparation, advanced field investigation, and guidance in preparation of museum material for publication.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ANT 003 recommended.

title: Prehistoric Technology: The Material Aspects of Prehistoric Adaptation
description: This version has ended; see updated course, below.Course Description: Examination of the role of lithic, ceramic, textile and wooden implements as elements in prehistoric survival and development. Emphasis is descriptive, but the significance of material resources as factors in prehistoric adaptation, settlement patterns, and culture change are discussed.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ANT 003 recommended.

title: Lithic Analysis
description: Course Description: Basic concepts of lithic analysis. General introduction on the place of stone tool technology in the archeological record. Physics, terminology and methodological concepts behind the study of stone tools. Review of the development of stone tool technology from its emergence.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ANT 003 recommended.

title: Cultural Resource Management in Archaeology
description: Course Description: Examination of legal foundations and goals of cultural resource management in the United States, with a focus on archaeological resources. Review of state and federal regulations, guidelines for assessing eligibility for listing in the National Register of Historic Places, professional practices to preserve and mitigate damage to resources, and public outreach practices.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ANT 003; or consent of instructor; ANT 170 recommended.

title: Forensic Archaeology Field Course
description: Course Description: Application of archaeological field methods to data recovery in forensic or medico-legal cases. Training in site survey, mapping, digitization, excavation, and evidence collection strategies. Field locations to be determined; international travel may be require
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ANT 003; consent of instructor; ANT 056 recommended.

title: Topics in Anthropology
description: Course Description: Intensive treatment of a special anthropological topic or problem.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Upper division standing.

title: Internship in Anthropology
description: Course Description: Work experience off and on campus in all subject areas offered in the Department of Anthropology under the supervision of a member of the faculty.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Consent of instructor; upper division standing.

title: Special Study for Honors Students
description: Course Description: Independent study of an anthropological problem involving the writing of an honors thesis.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Consent of instructor; open only to majors of senior standing who qualify for honors program.

title: Tutoring in Anthropology
description: Course Description: Leading of small voluntary discussion groups affiliated with one of the department's regular courses.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Upper division standing with major in Anthropology and consent of Department Chairperson.

title: Directed Group Study
description: Course Description: Directed reading and group discussion of selected anthropological problems.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Consent of instructor.

title: Special Study for Advanced Undergraduates
description: Course Description: Special study for advanced undergraduates.

title: History of Anthropology
description: Course Description: Historical development of socio-cultural theory within anthropology, from mid-19th to mid-20th centuries. Focus on original theory texts in context of historical developments in the field as a whole.

title: Critical Readings in Ethnography
description: Course Description: Critical readings of selected ethnographies that examine a wide range of important topics and analytical issues in social and cultural anthropology. Emphasis on how and why ethnographic writing has changed over time and its relationship with contemporary theoretical explorations.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Graduate student in Anthropology or consent of instructor.

title: History & Theory of Archaeology
description: Course Description: History of archaeology and archaeological theory and analysis of archaeological research methodology.

title: Contemporary Issues in Anthropological Theory
description: Course Description: Advanced consideration of fundamental issues in anthropological theory. Emphasis on critical examination of major contemporary debates between proponents of competing theories.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ANT 002; ANT 137; or consent of instructor.

title: History & Theory in Anthropological Linguistics
description: Course Description: History of thought in anthropological linguistics. Consideration of the historical development of fundamental ideas in anthropological linguistics, of major theoretical issues, and of research methodology.

title: Ethnographic Writing
description: Course Description: Relationship between conducting participant observation of others and writing it up,emphasizing the processual rift between the reality of fieldwork and its written representation. Study of various literary genres and textual strategies used in cultural anthropology.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ANT 137; ANT 201; or the equivalent.

title: Writing & Research Design in Evolutionary Anthropology
description: Course Description: Guided preparation of a Ph.D. dissertation proposal or MA thesis/report. Discussion of literature review, hypothesis testing, research design and grant writing as relates to anthropology. Culminates in an oral capstone presentation to the department

title: Aspects of Culture Structure
description: Course Description: Analysis of various phases of culture, such as religion, economics, law, and folklore.

title: Political Ecology
description: Course Description: Interdisciplinary seminar evaluating contributions from ecological anthropology, political economy, cultural constructivism, postmodernism, and feminism towards development of theories of political ecology. Historical relationships between local/global power structures, environmental degradation, and resistance movements. Case studies of desertification, deforestation, mining, conservation, development.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Graduate standing or consent of instructor.

title: Problems in Archeological Method
description: Course Description: Techniques for analyzing archeological data; application to various prehistoric cultures.

title: Quantitative Modeling in Archaeology
description: Course Description: Examination of the nature of archaeological data with a focus on the quantitative and statistical techniques available to model, analyze, display, and make sense of such data.

title: Topics in New World Prehistory
description: Course Description: Advanced study on current problems in New World Prehistory and archaeology.

title: Topics in Old World Prehistory
description: Course Description: Advanced study on current problems in Old World prehistory and archaeology.

title: Field Course in Linguistics
description: Course Description: Techniques of eliciting, recording, and analyzing; work with a native speaker.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ANT 110; ANT 111.

title: Rural Transformation in Postcolonial Societies
description: Course Description: Problems of rural transformation arising out of political and economic interaction between national elites and rural regional and local populations under varying conditions of induced change in postcolonial societies. Attention given to the implications of this interaction for rapid economic growth.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ANT 223; ANT 265; or consent of instructor.

title: Cities & Citizenship
description: Course Description: Explores the nature of modern cities, urban socioeconomic life, and urban culture and politics from an anthropological perspective.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Consent of instructor; graduate standing.

title: Economic Anthropology
description: Course Description: Selected current methodological and theoretical problems in the analysis of nonindustrial economic systems.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ANT 122; or consent of instructor.

title: Problems in Comparative Religion
description: Course Description: Advanced study of current problems in the anthropological study of religion.

title: State & Nation in the Modern World
description: Course Description: A presentation of current anthropological theories of the origins and nature of the modern nation-state in both the First and Third Worlds, with special reference to state ideology (nationalism) and forms of control.

title: Consciousness & Resistance
description: Course Description: Consideration of approaches to the study of social inequality, and responses of subordinated groups. Emphasis on situating approaches to contemporary social theory, concrete research problems, and political strategies. Topics: formation of consciousness and identity; collective action, accommodation to frontal resistance.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Completion of first-year graduate work or consent of instructor.

title: Culture & Power
description: Course Description: Exploration of one of the core paradigms within contemporary anthropological inquiry, "culture and power." Focus on how distinct theoretical perspectives–Marxism, post-Marxism, structuralism, post-structuralism, and feminism–have examined the mutually constitutive nature of culture and power.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Graduate standing or consent of instructor.

title: Gender, Identity, & Self
description: Course Description: Intersections of gender, identity, and selfhood cross-culturally and historically. How the self is feminized and masculinized, and interfaces with sexual, race, class, work, national, minority, and majority identities under different historical, cultural, and social structural conditions.

title: Family Systems & Reproduction: Theory & Comparisons
description: Course Description: Comparative examination of family systems in historical context and of reproductive behaviors and strategizing. A major theme is how family-system norms specify the relative desirability of differently configured offspring sets. Cases are drawn from Western Europe and South and East Asia.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Graduate standing in one of the social sciences including History.

title: Political Movements
description: Course Description: Interdisciplinary approach to political movements of protest, reform, and revolution emphasizing historical comparison and evaluation of major theoretical approaches including world systems, resource mobilization, state & culture, rational choice, moral economy, social class and gender.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Completion of first-year graduate work recommended.

title: Problems in African Society & Culture
description: Course Description: Diachronic analyses of traditional institutions in sub-Saharan Africa.

title: Topics in North American Ethnology
description: Course Description: Advanced study on current problems in North American ethnography and culture history.

title: Ethnology of Northern & Central Asia
description: Course Description: Lectures on the culture aboriginally found north of the Caucasus-Korea line. Supervised study of the primary and secondary sources. Work with informants when available.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Reading knowledge of German, Russian, Chinese, or Japanese.

title: Ethnology of Europe
description: Course Description: Supervised study of the primary and secondary sources dealing with the ethnography and ethnology of the peoples of Europe. Emphasis upon folk, peasant, and minority groups.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Reading knowledge of a European language other than English.

title: Topics in Chinese Culture & Society
description: Course Description: Selected topics in the anthropology of Chinese society. Focus on one or more of the following topics: state-society dynamics, family and gender, city formation and urban life, social movement, labor politics, and religion and ideology in Chinese society.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Graduate standing in the social sciences, history, or the humanities.

title: Behavioral Ecology of Primates
description: Course Description: Concepts, issues, and hypotheses in primate behavioral ecology, with emphasis on the social and ecological determinants and consequences of variation in social organization for individuals.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ANT 154A (can be concurrent); or the equivalent, graduate standing.

title: Human Evolution Seminar
description: Course Description: Study of selected topics in human evolutionary studies. Each year, focuses on one or more of the following: molecular evolution, primate evolutionary biology, Tertiary hominoids, Australopithecus, Homo erectus, archaic Homo sapiens, brain evolution.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ANT 152; and consent of instructor, or the equivalent of ANT 152.

title: Seminar in Human Genomics
description: Course Description: In-depth study of current topics in human genomics including genetic diversity, migration, phenotypic evolution and genetic associations with disease. Focus on population genetic theory. Topic changes yearly.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Consent of instructor.

title: Current Issues in Primate Sociobiology
description: Course Description: Analysis of primate behavior, with particular emphasis on preparation for field studies.

title: Primate Conservation Biology
description: Course Description: Application of understanding of primate biology to conservation of primates and their habitat. Topics include evolutionary anthropology, behavioral ecology, biogeography, macroecology, population biology, and socio-ecology of primates.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ANT 154; Graduate standing, or upper division undergraduates with consent of instructor.

title: Evolution & Human Behavior
description: Course Description: Exploration of the links between behavioral ecological theory and human cultural variation, focusing on reproduction, marriage, parental investment and family structure; implications of evolutionary theory for social organization in human communities, historical and contemporary.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Graduate standing, or consent of instructor.

title: Human Applications of Foraging Theory
description: Course Description: Foraging theory models and their use in ethnographic and archaeological analyses of human behavior, with a focus on hunter-gatherers and resource selection, patch use, population and habitat, central places, sharing, stochastic processes, population dynamics, and conservation behavior.

title: Language, Performance, & Power
description: Course Description: Exploration of the intersection between linguistic and social theories in the language-state relation and the performance of identity. Ideological sources of language differentiation; nation-building and linguistic difference. Political economic, sociolinguistic, and ethnographic approaches to understanding linguistic inequality.

title: Anthropology Colloquium Seminar
description: This version has ended; see updated course, below.Course Description: Reports and discussions of recent advances in the four subfields of anthropology. Presented by guest speakers.

title: Current Anthropology Journal Editorial Workshop
description: Course Description: Reading and offering workshop critiques of manuscripts submitted for publication, and reading and discussion of other relevant work in anthropology and human ecology. Track and edit published comments and authors’ replies that accompany major features. Participation in the development of new sections for the electronic edition of the journal, including a "news and views" section and a debate section.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Consent of instructor.

title: Advanced Topics in Human Behavioral Ecology
description: Course Description: Topically focused, critical discussion of current and emerging research in the field of human behavioral ecology, giving special attention to theory, concepts, models, and methods for the evolutionary analysis of ethnographic and archaeological evidence.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ANT 261 or ANT 262 or ANT 263; and consent of instructor, or comparable experience in anthropology, or related disciplines.

title: Seminar in Linguistic Anthropology
description: Course Description: Selected topics in linguistic anthropology.

title: Group Study
description: Course Description: Group study.

title: Research
description: Course Description: Research.

title: Dissertation Research
description: Course Description: Dissertation research.

title: Teaching Anthropology
description: Course Description: Intellectual and practical elements of college teaching in the field of Anthropology, from curriculum design and the syllabus through grading and course evaluations, including classroom and information technology methods, and problems and rewards of teaching in higher education.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Graduate standing in Anthropology or closely related discipline.

title: Teaching Assistant Training Practicum
description: Course Description: Teaching assistant training practicum.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Graduate standing.

title: Internship
description: Course Description: Internship experience off and on campus in all subject areas offered in the Department of Anatomy, Physiology & Cell Biology. Internships are supervised by a member of the faculty.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Consent of instructor; lower division standing.

title: Special Study for Undergraduates
description: Course Description: Special study for undergraduates.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Consent of instructor.

title: Comparative Vertebrate Organology
description: Course Description: Functional anatomy of major organ systems in vertebrates. Each system examined from cellular to gross level in fish, birds, and mammals. Emphasis on how differentiated cell types are integrated into tissues and organs to perform diverse physiological functions.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): (BIS 001A, BIS 001B) or (BIS 002A, BIS 002B).

title: Fundamentals of Immunology
description: Course Description: Fundamental concepts of immunology including components of the immune system, initiation and regulation of the immune response, infection and immunity, allergy and hypersensitivity, autoimmunity, and transplantation.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): BIS 102 or BIS 105 or ABI 102; or equivalent, or consent of instructor.

title: Internship
description: Course Description: Internship experience off and on campus in all subject areas offered in the Department of Anatomy, Physiology & Cell Biology. Internships are supervised by a member of the faculty.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Upper division standing; approval of internship.

title: Directed Group Study
description: Course Description: Directed group study.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Consent of instructor.

title: Special Study for Advanced Undergraduates
description: Course Description: Special study for advanced undergraduates.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Consent of instructor.

title: Basics of Microscopy & Cellular Imaging
description: Course Description: Practical applications of basic microscope techniques used to image cells and tissues with the goal of using these techniques to generate publication quality images. Principles of light, epifluorescent, confocal and electron microscopy, their applications and limitations.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Consent of instructor; graduate standing.

title: Seminar
description: Course Description: Discussion and critical evaluation of advanced topics and current trends in research.

title: Topics in Biology of Respiratory System
description: Course Description: Topics concerning structure and function of respiratory system. Possible topics include: lung growth, pulmonary reaction to toxicants, pulmonary inflammation, lung metabolism, biology of lung cells, tracheobronchial epithelium, nasal cavity structure and function.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Consent of instructor; graduate standing.

title: Group Study
description: Course Description: Group study.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Consent of instructor.

title: Research
description: Course Description: Research.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Consent of instructor.

title: Elementary Arabic 1
description: Course Description: Introduction to basic Arabic. Interactive and integrated presentation of listening, speaking, reading, and writing skills, including the alphabet and basic syntax. Focus on standard Arabic with basic skills in spoken Egyptian and/or one other colloquial dialect.

title: Accelerated Intensive Elementary Arabic
description: This version has ended; see updated course, below.Course Description: Special 12-week accelerated, intensive summer session course that combines the work of courses ARB 001, 002, and 003. Introduction to Modern Standard Arabic through development of all language skills in a cultural context with emphasis on communicative proficiency.

title: Elementary Arabic 2
description: Course Description: Continuation of basic Arabic from ARB 001. Interactive and integrated presentation of listening, speaking, reading, and writing skills, including syntax. Focus on standard Arabic and limited use of spoken Egyptian and/or one other colloquial dialect.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ARB 001; or consent of instructor.

title: Elementary Arabic 3
description: Course Description: Continuation of introduction to basic Arabic from ARB 001 and ARB 002. Interactive and integrated presentation of listening, speaking, reading, and writing skills, including syntax. Focus on standard Arabic with limited use of spoken Egyptian and/or one other colloquial dialect.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ARB 002; or consent of instructor.

title: Intermediate Arabic 21
description: Course Description: Builds on courses ARB 001, ARB 002, and ARB 003. Interactive and integrated presentation of listening, speaking, reading, and writing skills, including idiomatic expression. Focus on standard Arabic with limited use of Egyptian and/or one other colloquial dialect.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ARB 003; or consent of instructor.

title: Accelerated Intensive Intermediate Arabic
description: This version has ended; see updated course, below.Course Description: Special 12-week accelerated, intensive summer session course that combines the work of courses ARB 021, ARB 022, and ARB 023. Modern Standard Arabic through development of all language skills in a cultural context with emphasis on communicative proficiency.

title: Colloquial Egyptian Arabic
description: Course Description: Continuation of the Colloquial Egyptian Arabic covered in the first year of Arabic; ARB 001, ARB 002, and ARB 003.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ARB 003; or consent of instructor.

title: Colloquial Levantine Arabic
description: Course Description: Continuation of colloquial Levantine Arabic presented in ARB 001, ARB 002 and ARB 003. Integrated presentation of speaking and listening skills in colloquial Levantine Arabic, with reading and writing in Modern Standard Arabic that is related to Levantine cultural production and social life.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ARB 003; or consent of instructor.

title: Intermediate Arabic 22
description: Course Description: Continuation of ARB 021. Interactive and integrated presentation of listening, speaking, reading, and writing, including idiomatic expression. Focus on standard Arabic with limited use of Egyptian and/or one other colloquial dialect.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ARB 021; or consent of instructor.

title: Colloquial Egyptian Arabic
description: Course Description: Continuation of the Colloquial Egyptian Arabic covered in first year of Arabic; courses ARB 001, ARB 002, and ARB 003 and the first quarter of Colloquial Arabic ARB 021C.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ARB 021C; or consent of instructor.

title: Colloquial Levantine Arabic
description: Course Description: Continuation of colloquial Levantine Arabic presented in ARB 021L. Integrated presentation of speaking and listening skills in colloquial Levantine Arabic; reading and writing in Modern Standard Arabic related to Levantine social life.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ARB 021L; or consent of instructor.

title: Intermediate Arabic 23
description: Course Description: Continuation of ARB 021 and ARB 022. Interactive and integrated presentation of Arabic listening, speaking, reading, and writing skills, including idiomatic expression.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ARB 022; or consent of instructor.

title: Colloquial Egyptian Arabic
description: Course Description: Continuation of Colloquial Egyptian Arabic covered in ARB 022C.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ARB 022C; or consent of instructor.

title: Colloquial Levantine Arabic
description: Course Description: Continuation of colloquial Levantine Arabic presented in ARB 022L. Integrated presentation of speaking and listening skills in colloquial Levantine Arabic; reading and writing in Modern Standard Arabic related to Levantine social life.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ARB 022L; or consent of instructor.

title: Arabic Tutoring
description: Course Description: Tutoring in undergraduate courses, including leadership in small voluntary discussion groups affiliated with departmental courses.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ARB 023 B or better; consent of Program Director required.

title: Directed Group Study
description: Course Description: Directed group study.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Consent of instructor.

title: Special Study for Undergraduates
description: Course Description: Special study.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Consent of instructor.

title: Readings in Arabic: 600-1850
description: This version has ended; see updated course, below.Course Description: Readings in Arabic. Poetry, prose literature, and selections from texts on religion, history, politics, science, philosophy and mysticism.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ARB 123; or consent of instructor.

title: Advanced Arabic
description: Course Description: Review, refinement, and development of skills learned in intermediate Arabic through work with texts, video, and audio on cultural and social issues. Integrated approach to reading, writing, listening, speaking primarily standard Arabic, with limited use of one colloquial dialect.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ARB 023; or consent of instructor.

title: Advanced Arabic
description: Course Description: Continuation of ARB 121. Further development of advanced skills in reading, listening, writing, and speaking standard Arabic through work with texts, video, and audio on cultural and social issues. Limited use of one colloquial dialect.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ARB 121; or consent of instructor.

title: Advanced Arabic
description: Course Description: Continuation of ARB 122. Further development of advanced skills in reading, listening, writing, and speaking standard Arabic through work with texts, video, and audio on cultural and social issues. Limited use of one colloquial dialect.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ARB 122; or consent of instructor.

title: A Story for a Life: The Arabian Nights
description: Course Description: In-depth exploration of The Arabian Nights, the best-known work of pre-modern Arabic literature and a major work of world literature. Analysis of the work in its historical context and in comparison to other frame tales in world literature.

title: Readings in Modern Arabic Literature
description: This version has ended; see updated course, below.Course Description: Readings of modern Arabic poetry and fiction in original format, assisted by instructor-prepared glossaries and other supplementary material. Readings to be followed by class discussion and short writing assignments in Arabic. Open to students at advanced proficiency in Arabic.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ARB 123; or consent of instructor.

title: Tutoring in Arabic
description: Course Description: Tutoring in undergraduate courses, including leadership in small voluntary discussion groups affiliated with departmental courses.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ARB 023 B or better; consent of Program Director.

title: Directed Group Study
description: Course Description: Development of reading, writing, speaking, and listening skills in advanced Arabic. Materials may include al-Kitaab Part Two or Three, news articles and broadcasts, short stories, poetry, novels, essays, scripture, prophetic traditions, audio recordings, and television and film.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Consent of instructor.

title: Directed Independent Study
description: Course Description: Directed Independent Study on a topic culminating in a term paper. Independent Study may only be arranged with consent of the instructor when graduate seminars are unavailable. Topic varies by instructor.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Graduate standing or consent of instructor.

title: Individual Study
description: Course Description: Individual study.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Consent of instructor; graduate standing.

title: Teaching Assistant Training Practicum
description: Course Description: Teaching assistant training practicum.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Consent of instructor. Graduate standing.

title: Economic Basis of the Agricultural Industry
description: Course Description: Agriculture and man; the agricultural industry in U.S. and world economies; production and supply, marketing and demand; agricultural land, capital and labor markets; economic and social problems of agriculture in an urban and industrialized economy emphasizing California. Not currently offered.

title: Economic Basis of the Agricultural Industry
description: Course Description: Agriculture and man; the agricultural industry in Australia and world economies; production and supply, marketing and demand; agricultural land, capital and labor markets; economic and social problems of agriculture in an urban and industrialized economy emphasizing Australia. Not currently offered.

title: Population, Environment & World Agriculture
description: Course Description: Economic analysis of interactions among population, environment, natural resources and development of world agriculture. Introduces students to economic thinking about population growth, its causes and consequences for world food demand, and environmental and technological limits to increasing food supplies. Not currently offered.

title: Business Law
description: Course Description: General principles of business law in the areas of contracts, business organization, real property, uniform commercial code, sales, commercial paper, employment relations, and creditor-debtor against a background of the history and functioning of our present legal system.

title: Directed Group Study
description: Course Description: Directed group study.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Consent of instructor.

title: Special Study for Undergraduates
description: Course Description: Special study for undergraduates.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Consent of instructor.

title: Intermediate Microeconomics: Theory of Production & Consumption
description: Course Description: Theory of individual consumer and market demand; theory of production and supply of agricultural products, with particular reference to the individual firm; price determination, and employment of resources under pure competition.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): (ECN 001A C- or better or ECN 001AY C- or better or ECN 001AV C- or better); (ECN 001B C- or better or ECN 001BV C- or better); ((MAT 019A C- or better, MAT 019B C- or better, MAT 019C C- or better) or (MAT 017A C- or better, MAT 017B C- or better) or (MAT 021A C- or better, MAT 021B C- or better)).

title: Intermediate Microeconomics: Imperfect Competition, Markets & Welfare Economics
description: Course Description: Price determination, and employment of resources under conditions of monopoly, oligopoly, and monopolistic competition.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ARE 100A C- or better; ((ECN 001A C- or better or ECN 001AY C- or better or ECN 001AV C- or better); ECN 001B C- or better or ECN 001BV C- or better); ((MAT 016A C- or better, MAT 016B C- or better, MAT 016C C- or better) or (MAT 017A C- or better, MAT 017B C- or better) or (MAT 021A C- or better, MAT 021B C- or better)); ARE 018 C- or better.

title: Econometric Theory & Applications
description: This version has ended; see updated course, below.Course Description: Statistical methods for analyzing data to solve problems in managerial economics. Topics include the linear regression model, methods to resolve data problems, and the economic interpretation of results.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ARE 100A C- or better; (STA 013 C- or better or STA 013Y C- or better); STA 103 C- or better.

title: Econometrics for Business Decisions
description: Course Description: Covers state-of-the art econometric and statistical methods for causal and predictive modeling with applications to finance, marketing, and public policy.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ARE 106 C- or better; STA 103 C- or better.

title: Fundamentals of Organization Management
description: Course Description: Role of organizational design and behavior in business and public agencies. Principles of planning, decision making, individual behavior, management, leadership, informal groups, conflict and change in the organization.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Upper division standing recommended.

title: Fundamentals of Marketing Management
description: Course Description: Nature of product marketing by the business firm. Customer-product relationships, pricing and demand; new product development and marketing strategy; promotion and advertising; product life cycles; the distribution system; manufacturing, wholesaling, retailing. Government regulation and restraints.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ECN 001A or ECN 001AY or ECN 001AV. For non-majors only.

title: Economic Development
description: Course Description: Major issues encountered in emerging from international poverty, problems of growth and structural change, human welfare, population growth and health, labor markets and internal migration. Important issues of policy concerning international trade and industrialization.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): (ECN 001A or ECN 001AY or ECN 001AV); (ECN 001B or ECN 001BV).

title: Economic Development
description: Course Description: Macroeconomic issues of developing countries. Issues include problems in generating capital, conduct of monetary and fiscal policies, foreign aid and investment. Important issues of policy concerning international borrowing and external debt of developing countries. May be taught abroad.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): (ECN 001A or ECN 001AY or ECN 001AV); (ECN 001B or ECN 001BV).

title: Economic Development
description: Course Description: Macroeconomic issues of developing countries. Problems in generating capital, conduct of monetary and fiscal policies, foreign aid and investment. Issues of policy concerning international borrowing and external debt of developing countries.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): (ECN 001A or ECN 001AY or ECN 001AV); (ECN 001B or ECN 001BV).

title: Tax Accounting
description: Course Description: Development and application of a framework to understand the tax effects of typical management decisions on both entities and their owners. Impacts that different methods of taxation have on business entities with emphasis on tax planning, using income and deduction strategies, retirement plans, and choice of business entity for tax minimization. Irregularly offered.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): (MGT 011A C- or better or MGT 011AV C- or better or MGT 011AY C- or better); MGT 011B C- or better; ARE 018 recommended.

title: Intermediate Managerial Accounting
description: Course Description: Use of accounting information by mangers in decision making, planning, directing and controlling operations. Focuses on managerial/cost accounting theory and practice. Covers costing systems, budgeting, and financial statement analysis.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ( MGT 011A C- or better or MGT 011AV C- or better or MGT 011AY C- or better); MGT 011B C- or better.

title: Agricultural Policy
description: Course Description: Analytical treatment of historical and current economic problems and governmental policies influencing American agriculture. Uses of economic theory to develop historical and conceptual understanding of the economics of agriculture; how public policy influences the nature and performance of American agriculture.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ARE 100A C- or better.

title: Agricultural Policy
description: Course Description: Analytical treatment of historical and current economic problems and governmental policies influencing agriculture. Uses of economic theory to develop historical and conceptual understanding of the economics of agriculture; how public policy influences the nature and performance of agriculture. Not currently offered.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ARE 100A; or consent of instructor.

title: Economics of Agricultural Sustainability
description: Course Description: Application of economic concepts to agro-environmental issues relevant to agricultural sustainability. Topics include market efficiency, production externalities, government policies, agricultural trade, product differentiation, all linked to sustainability issues. Case studies include biofuels, genetically modified foods and geographically differentiated products.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ECN 001A C- or better or ECN 001AY C- or better. or ECN 001AV C- or better.

title: Agricultural Markets
description: This version has ended; see updated course, below.Course Description: Nature, function, organizational structure, and operation of agricultural markets; prices, costs, and margins; market information, regulation, and controls; cooperative marketing. Irregularly offered.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ARE 106 C- or better; ARE 100A C- or better; (STA 013 C- or better or STA 013Y C- or better); STA 103 C- or better.

title: Cooperative Business Enterprises
description: Course Description: Study of cooperative business enterprise in the United States and elsewhere; economic theories of behavior, principles of operation, finance, decision-making, and taxation.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ECN 001A C- or better or ECN 001AY C- or better or ECN 001AV C- or better.

title: Introduction to Behavioral Economics
description: This version has ended; see updated course, below.Course Description: Inclusion of non-economic factors such as psychological principles in economic decisions and model predictions. Emphasis on behavioral principles, resulting strategies and implications for diverse market settings.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): (ECN 001A C- or better or ECN 001AY C- or better or ECN 001AV C- or better); (STA 013 C- or better or STA 013Y C- or better).

title: Agribusiness Marketing Plan Development
description: Course Description: Fundamental components required to develop a marketing plan. Appreciation of the concept of marketing plans, appropriate research required, including the use of library, Internet, survey and interview instruments, government documents, market analysis, business proposition, action planning, financial evaluation and monitoring.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Upper division standing.

title: Managerial Marketing
description: Course Description: Application of economic theory and econometrics to the study of marketing and consumer research. Emphasis on industry structure, history, regulatory aspects, integrated brand promotion, market segmentation, optimal product mix, message placement.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): (ARE 100B C- or better or ECN 100B C- or better); ARE 106 C- or better; STA 103 C- or better.

title: Wine Business & Economics
description: Course Description: Business plan development. Vineyard strategies and climate change. Evaluation of financials in the wine industry context for strategic decisions and forecasting. Wine industry business models. Integrating production, price point, distribution and marketing strategies. Inventory management. World wine markets.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): (ECN 001A C- or better or ECN 001AV C- or better or ECN 001AY C- or better); (MGT 011A C- or better or MGT 011AV C- or better or MGT 011AY C- or better); MGT 011B C- or better; VEN 003 C- or better; (VEN 111 (can be concurrent) or VEN 170 (can be concurrent) or ARE 113 (can be concurrent) or ARE 135 (can be concurrent) or ARE 136 (can be concurrent)).

title: International Commodity & Resource Markets
description: Course Description: Basic nature and scope of international trade in agricultural commodities, agricultural inputs, and natural resources. Market dimensions and policy institutions. Case studies to illustrate import and export problems associated with different regions and commodities.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ARE 100A C- or better; ARE 100B C- or better.

title: Futures & Options Markets
description: Course Description: History, mechanics, and economic functions of futures and options markets; hedging; theory of inter-temporal price formation and behavior of futures and options prices; price forecasting; futures and options as policy tools.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): (ARE 100A C- or better or ECN 100A C- or better or ECN 100AV C- or better); STA 103 C- or better.

title: Farm Management
description: Course Description: Farm organization and resources; economic and technological principles in decision making; analytical techniques and management control; problems in organizing and managing the farm business. Irregularly offered.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ECN 001A C- or better or ECN 001AY C- or better or ECN 001AV C- or better.

title: Personal Finance
description: Course Description: Management of income and expenditures by the household. Use of consumer credit, savings, and insurance by households. Principles of tax, retirement, and estate planning.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ECN 001B or ECN 001BV.

title: Basics of Investing
description: Course Description: Survey of investment institutions, sources of investment information, and portfolio theory. Analysis of the stock, bond and real estate markets from the perspective of the investor.

title: Real Estate Economics
description: Course Description: Economic theory, analysis, and institutions of real estate markets and related financial markets. Techniques for appraising property values. Cases drawn from the raw land, single family, multifamily, industrial and office real estate markets.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ARE 100A C- or better.

title: Farm & Rural Resources Appraisal
description: Course Description: Principles and procedures of the valuation process with emphasis placed on rural real estate. Includes identification of the major physical and economic determinants of value, the three primary appraisal approaches to valuation, discussion of appraisal activity and practice.

title: Economics of Business Regulation
description: Course Description: Variety, nature and impact of government regulation: anti-trust laws and economic and social regulation. Nature of the legislative process, promulgation of regulations, and their impact, especially as analyzed by economists.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ARE 100B C- or better.

title: Resource & Environment Policy Analysis
description: Course Description: Natural resource use problems with emphasis on past and current policies and institutions affecting resource use; determinants, principles, and patterns of natural resource use; property rights; conservation; private and public resource use problems; and public issues. Not currently offered.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ECN 001A or ECN 001AY or ECN 001AV.

title: Resource & Environmental Policy Analysis
description: Course Description: Natural resource use problems with emphasis on past and current policies and institutions affecting resource use; determinants, principles, and patterns of natural resource use; property rights; conservation; private and public resource use problems; and public issues. Not currently offered.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ECN 001A or ECN 001AY or ECN 001AV.

title: Agricultural Labor
description: Course Description: Analysis of labor markets with focus on U.S. and world agriculture. Labor supply, demand, market equilibrium; why farm labor markets are different; global trends in farm labor; U.S. farm labor history; unions and collective bargaining; immigration policy.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ARE 100A C- or better.

title: Operations Research & Management Science
description: This version has ended; see updated course, below.Course Description: Introduction to quantitative methods used to analyze business and economic processes: decision analysis for management, mathematical programming, competitive analysis, and other methods.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ARE 100A C- or better; (STA 013 C- or better or STA 013Y C- or better); STA 103 C- or better.

title: Introduction to Mathematical Economics
description: This version has ended; see updated course, below.Course Description: Linear algebra for economists; necessary and sufficient conditions in static optimization problems; implicit function theorem; economic methodology and mathematics; comparative statics; envelope theorem; Le Chatelier principle; applications to production and consumer models. Not currently offered.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ARE 100B; ARE 155; ARE 100A C- or better; (STA 013 C- or better or STA 013Y C- or better); STA 103 C- or better.

title: Analysis for Operations & Production Management
description: This version has ended; see updated course, below.Course Description: Application of economic theory and quantitative methods to analyze operations and production management problems including process strategy, quality management, location and plant layout, and inventory management.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ARE 155 C- or better; ARE 100A C- or better; (STA 013 C- or better or STA 013Y C- or better); STA 103 C- or better.

title: Emerging Economies & Globalization
description: Course Description: Economic drivers and policy challenges in the major emerging markets, with an emphasis on the effects of rising incomes, population growth, urbanization, and relative wages on world markets and natural resources. Not currently offered.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ARE 100A C- or better; ARE 115A; (ARE 115B or ARE 115BY); completion of ARE 106 & ECN 162 strongly recommended.

title: Economics of Global Poverty Reduction: What Works & Why
description: Course Description: Application of microeconomic theory and econometrics to understand causes of poverty and critically evaluate poverty alleviation policies in low income countries. Irregularly offered.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): (ARE 100B C- or better or ECN 100B C- or better); (ARE 106 C- or better or ECN 140 C- or better); (ARE 115A C- or better or ECN 115A C- or better).

title: Principles of Finance
description: Course Description: Principles of corporate financial management. Time value of money, interest rates, principles of valuation, NPV, risk and return, and cost of capital.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ARE 100A C- or better; ARE 106 C- or better; (MGT 011A C- or better or MGT 011AV C- or better or MGT 011AY C- or better); MGT 011B C- or better.

title: Corporate Finance
description: Course Description: Financial analysis at the firm level: optimizing capital structure; minimizing the cost of capital; dividend policies; mergers and acquisitions; real options; and risk management.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ARE 171 C- or better; ARE 106 C- or better; (MGT 011A C- or better or MGT 011AV C- or better or MGT 011AY C- or better); MGT 011B C- or better.

title: Investments
description: Course Description: Introduction to asset pricing. Valuation and risk characteristics of financial assets, including stocks, bonds, futures, and options. Investors’ attitudes toward risk, capital allocation, portfolio selection, the capital asset pricing model, and the efficient market hypothesis.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ARE 171 C- or better; ARE 106 C- or better; (MGT 011A C- or better or MGT 011AV C- or better or MGT 011AY C- or better); MGT 011B C- or better.

title: Natural Resource Economics
description: Course Description: Economic concepts and policy issues associated with natural resources, renewable resources (ground water, forests, fisheries, and wildlife populations) and non-renewable resources (minerals and energy resources, soil).
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ARE 100A C- or better or ECN 100A C- or better or ECN 100AV C- or better or ECN 100 C- or better.

title: Environmental Economics
description: Course Description: Role of the environment in economic activity and methods for protecting and enhancing environmental quality; implications of market failures for public policy; design of environmental policy; theory of welfare measurement; measuring the benefits of environmental improvement.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ARE 100A C- or better or ECN 100A C- or better or ECN 100AV C- or better or ECN 100 C- or better.

title: Topics in Managerial Economics
description: Course Description: Selected topics in managerial economics, focusing on current research.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ARE 100A; STA 103; and consent of instructor.

title: Internship
description: Course Description: Internship experience off and on campus in all subject areas offered in the Department of Agricultural & Resource Economics. Internships are supervised by a member of the staff.

title: Senior Research Thesis
description: Course Description: Independent study program and seminar on research methods in applied economics. Supervised completion of a first draft of a research thesis.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ARE 100B C- or better; ARE 106 C- or better; ARE 155 C- or better; consent of instructor; application required.

title: Senior Research Thesis
description: Course Description: Independent study program supervised by faculty mentor and culminating in the completion of a senior research thesis in applied economics.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ARE 194HA C- or better; ARE 107 (can be concurrent); consent of instructor.

title: Tutoring in Managerial Economics
description: Course Description: Undergraduates assist the instructor by tutoring students in one of the department's regularly scheduled courses.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Senior standing in Managerial Economics and consent of Department Chairperson.

title: Directed Group Study
description: Course Description: Directed group study. May be taught abroad.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Consent of instructor.

title: Special Study for Advanced Undergraduates
description: Course Description: Special study for advanced undergraduates. May be taught abroad.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Consent of instructor.

title: Microeconomic Theory
description: Course Description: Linear and non-linear optimization theory applied to develop the theory of the profit-maximizing firm and the utility-maximizing consumer.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Graduate standing.

title: Microeconomic Theory
description: Course Description: Characteristics of market equilibrium under perfect competition, simple monopoly and monopsony. Emphasis on general equilibrium and welfare economics; the sources of market success and market failure.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ARE 200A.

title: Microeconomic Theory
description: This version has ended; see updated course, below.Course Description: Uncertainty and information economics. Individual decision making under uncertainty. Introduction to game theory, with emphasis on applications to markets with firms that are imperfect competitors or consumers that are imperfectly informed.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ARE 200B.

title: Introduction to Applied Research Methods
description: Course Description: Study of philosophy and methodology of applied research in agricultural economics. Methods of conceptualization of researchable topics. Method of communication and constructive criticism.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ARE 204A; ARE 200A (can be concurrent); ARE 256A.

title: Applied Microeconomics: Consumer & Producer Behavior
description: Course Description: Application of consumer and producer theory in models of individual behavior and market-level phenomena. Implications of consumer and producer theory for specification of empirical models of supply and demand for inputs and outputs and market equilibrium displacement models.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ARE 200A; ARE 200B (can be concurrent); ARE 202A.

title: Research Design for Applied Microeconomics
description: Course Description: Third of three courses in the Ph.D. level applied microeconomics sequence. Examines the design of empirical research and the application of econometric theory.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ARE 202B; ARE 240A.

title: Microeconomic Analysis I
description: Course Description: Behavior of consumers and producers and their interactions; tools and methods needed to analyze economic behavior in the marketplace. Application of those methods to real-world problems.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ARE 100B or ECN 100; advanced undergraduates with consent of instructor.

title: Microeconomic Analysis II
description: Course Description: Behavior in imperfectly competitive markets–monopoly and price discrimination; oligopoly. Introduction to noncooperative game theory. Analysis of decisions made under risk and uncertainty and imperfect information. The economics of externalities and public goods.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ARE 204A; or consent of instructor.

title: Development Economics
description: Course Description: Review of the principal theoretical and empirical issues whose analysis has formed development economics. Analysis of economic development theories and development strategies and their application to specific policy issues in developing country contexts.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ARE 100A; ARE 100B; ECN 101; ARE 204A and ECN 160A, ECN 160B recommended.

title: Microdevelopment Theory & Methods I
description: Course Description: Agricultural development theory, with a focus on microeconomics. Agricultural household behavior with and without imperfections and uncertainty. Analysis of rural land, labor, credit and insurance markets, institutions, and contracts.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ARE 200A or ARE 204A; ARE 240A recommended.

title: Open Macroeconomics of Development
description: Course Description: Models and policy approaches regarding trade, monetary and fiscal issues, capital flows and debt are discussed in the macroeconomic framework of an open developing country. Basic analytical focus is real exchange rate and its impact on sectoral allocation of resources.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): (ARE 200A or ARE 204A); (ARE 214 or ARE 215A); (ARE 200D or ARE 205).

title: Microdevelopment Theory & Methods II
description: Course Description: Extension of development theory and microeconomic methods. Agricultural growth and technological change; poverty and income inequality; multisectoral, including village and regional models. Computable general equilibrium methods and applications.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ARE 215A.

title: Environmental & Economic Development
description: Course Description: Interdisciplinary course drawing on theoretical and empirical research on interactions between environmental resource use and economic development processes. Analysis of issues emerging at the interface of environmental and development economics.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ARE 200A; (ARE 204A or ARE 275).

title: International Agricultural Trade & Policy
description: Course Description: Analysis of country interdependence through world agricultural markets. Partial equilibrium analysis is used to study the impacts of national intervention on world markets, national policy choice in an open economy and multinational policy issues.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): (ARE 100B or ARE 204A); ECN 160A; or the equivalent of ECN 160A.

title: Economics of Agriculture
description: Course Description: Analytic treatment of the historical development and contemporary role of agriculture in the global, U.S. and California economies. Uses economic reasoning and evidence to develop historical and conceptual understanding of the economics of agriculture, agricultural issues, and related government policies.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ARE 204A (can be concurrent); ARE 256A (can be concurrent); or equivalent course(s) completed or concurrent required.

title: Supply & Demand for Agricultural Products
description: Course Description: Analysis of supply and demand for agricultural commodities emphasizing the effective use of microeconomic theory with econometric methods, and other empirical procedures, in conducting applied analysis of supply and demand at the firm and industry level.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ARE 200A; ARE 202A; ARE 240A; or consent of instructor.

title: Agricultural Commodity Markets
description: Course Description: Economic analysis of industries that produce, market, transport, store, and process basic commodities. Analysis of market equilibrium under perfect and imperfect competition, with and without government involvement.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ARE 200A; ARE 202A; ARE 240A; or consent of instructor.

title: Agricultural Policy
description: Course Description: Nature, formation, evolution, and institutions of economic policy applied to food, agricultural, and rural issues. Examples for detailed consideration include food security, commodity issues, and trade policy. Analytical approaches include static and dynamic welfare analysis, policy design, and political-economic analysis.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ARE 200A; ARE 202A; ARE 240A; or consent of instructor.

title: Econometric Foundations
description: Course Description: Prepares students for econometric theory and empirical work by examining the statistical foundation of econometrics. Special attention is paid to problems specific to non-experimental data common to social sciences. Topics from matrix algebra are also covered.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Graduate standing or consent of instructor.

title: Econometric Methods
description: Course Description: Least squares, instrumental variables, and maximum likelihood estimation and inference for single equation linear regression model; linear restrictions; heteroskedasticity; autocorrelation; lagged dependent variables.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ARE 239; or consent of instructor.

title: Econometric Methods
description: Course Description: Topics include asymptotic theory and instrumental variables, pooled time-series cross-section estimation, seemingly unrelated regression, classical hypothesis tests, identification and estimation of simultaneous equation models, cointegration, errorcorrection models, and qualitative and limited dependent variable models.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ARE 240A.

title: Time Series Econometrics
description: Course Description: Probability theory; estimation, inference and forecasting of time series models; trends and non-standard asymptotic theory; vector time series methods and cointegration; time series models for higher order moments and transition data; state-space modeling; the Kalman filter.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ARE 240B; or consent of instructor.

title: Cross Section Econometrics
description: Course Description: Estimation and inference for nonlinear regression models for cross-section data; models for discrete data and for limited dependent variables; models for panel data; additional topics such as bootstrap and semiparametric regression.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ARE 240B; or consent of instructor.

title: Topics in Time Series Econometrics
description: Course Description: Modern econometric techniques for time series data. Expand on topics covered in ECN 240A, ECN 240B and ECN 240C. Contents vary from year to year.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ARE 240C; or consent of instructor.

title: Topics in Cross Section Econometrics
description: Course Description: Modern econometrics techniques for cross-section data. Expand on topics covered in ECN 240A, ECN 240B and ECN 240D. Contents vary from year to year.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ARE 240D; or consent of instructor.

title: Optimization with Economic Applications
description: Course Description: Microeconomic topics in the framework of mathematical programming.

title: Dynamic Optimization Techniques with Economic Applications
description: Course Description: Necessary and sufficient conditions in the calculus of variations and optimal control, economic interpretations, the dynamic envelope theorem and transversality conditions, infinite horizon problems and phase diagrams, local stability and comparative statics of the steady state, comparative dynamics.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Elementary knowledge of ordinary differential equations.

title: Applied Dynamic Structural Econometric Modeling
description: Course Description: Covers structural econometric models of static games of incomplete information, single-agent dynamic optimization problems and multi-agent dynamic games, with a focus on applications to issues relevant to the environment, energy, natural resources, agriculture, and development.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ARE 254.

title: Applied Econometrics I
description: Course Description: First of two courses in the Masters-level econometrics sequence. The linear regression model and generalizations are applied to topics in applied economics. Tools for empirical research for problems requiring more sophisticated tools than standard regression models are emphasized.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ARE 106 or ECN 140; or consent of instructor.

title: Applied Econometrics II
description: Course Description: Second of two courses in the Masters-level econometrics sequence. The linear regression model and generalizations are applied to a variety of topics in applied economics. Tools for empirical research for problems requiring more sophisticated tools than standard regression models are emphasized.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ARE 256A; or consent of instructor.

title: Demand & Market Analysis
description: Course Description: Application of theoretical material covered in ECN 204A and ECN 204B, with particular focus on production theory/factor demand and imperfect competition/market power. Use of theoretical models as a foundation for empirical economic analysis, and empirical exercises. Independent research on chosen topics, with empirical application.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ARE 204B; ARE 256B; or consent of instructor.

title: Financial Economics
description: Course Description: Risk, aversion, choice under uncertainty. Arrow-Debreu securities. Valuation and risk characteristics of financial assets, capital allocation, portfolio selection, Capital Asset Pricing Model, efficient market hypothesis. Applications to topics in agricultural, resource and development economics.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ARE 204A; ARE 256A.

title: Economic Analysis of Resource & Environmental Policies
description: Course Description: Development of externality theory, market failure concepts, welfare economics, theory of renewable and non-renewable resource use, and political economic models. Applications to policy issues regarding the agricultural/environment interface and managing resources in the public domain.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): ARE 204A.

title: Environmental Economics: Externalities
description: Course Description: Introduces fundamental and recent research in environmental economics, focusing on the design, implementation and evaluation of environmental policy instruments to correct market failures. Exposure to economic theories and empirical techniques frequently used in this field.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Students should have completed the first year graduate level sequence in microeconomics and econometrics.

title: Environmental Economics: Non-Market Valuation
