title: Advanced Chemical Research Methodology
description: Course Description: Applications of the methodology developed in CHE 263 to experimental and theoretical studies. Advanced methods of interpretation of results are developed. Includes the preparation of manuscripts for publication.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): CHE 263; or consent of instructor.

title: Seminar
description: Course Description: Seminar.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Consent of instructor.

title: Presentation of Chemistry Research
description: Course Description: Introduces first- and second-year Chemistry graduate students to the process of giving an effective research presentation. Advanced Ph.D. students give formal seminars describing the design and execution of their research projects.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Graduate standing.

title: Careers in Chemistry
description: Course Description: Designed to give Chemistry graduate students an in-depth appreciation of career opportunities with a M.S. or Ph.D. degree in chemistry. Professional chemists (and allied professionals) give seminars describing both research and career insights.

title: Research in Pharmaceutical Chemistry
description: Course Description: Laboratory provides qualified graduate students with the opportunity to pursue original investigation in Pharmaceutical Chemistry and allied fields in order to fulfill the letter-graded research requirement of the Integrated B.S./M.S. Program in Chemistry (Pharmaceutical Chemistry Emphasis).
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): CHE 130A; CHE 130B; CHE 135; CHE 233 (can be concurrent); and consent of instructor.

title: Group Study
description: Course Description: Group study.

title: Research
description: Course Description: The laboratory is open to qualified graduate students who wish to pursue original investigation. Students wishing to enroll should communicate with the department well in advance of the quarter in which the work is to be undertaken.

title: Methods of Teaching Chemistry
description: Course Description: Practical experience in methods and problems of teaching chemistry. Includes analyses of texts and supporting material, discussion of teaching techniques, preparing for and conducting of discussion sessions and student laboratories. Participation in the teaching program required for Ph.D. in chemistry.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Consent of instructor; graduate student standing in Chemistry.

title: Advanced Methods of Teaching Chemistry
description: Course Description: Advanced topics in teaching chemistry. Analysis and discussion of curricular design, curricula materials, teaching methods and evaluation. For students who are planning a career in teaching chemistry.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): CHE 390.

title: Introduction to Chicana/o Studies
description: Course Description: Analysis of the situation of the Chicana/o (Mexican-American) people, emphasizing their history, literature, political movements, education and related areas.

title: Introduction to Chicana/o Studies
description: Course Description: Analysis of the situation of the Chicana/o (Mexican-American) people, emphasizing their history, literature, political movements, education and related areas.

title: Chicana/o & Latina/o Health Care Issues
description: This version has ended; see updated course, below.Course Description: Overview of health issues of Chicanas/os and Latinas/os in the State of California; role of poverty/lack of education in limited access to health care.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): CHI 010.

title: Chicana/o & Latina/o Health Care Issues
description: Course Description: Overview of health issues of Chicanas/os and Latinas/os in the State of California; role of poverty/lack of education and limited access to health care. All course instruction in Spanish. May be taught abroad.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): SPA 003 or SPA 003V or SPA 003Y; or equivalent.

title: Qualitative Research Methods
description: Course Description: Dominant models of qualitative inquiry in educational and social science research as well as mestizo approaches to research with latinos. Emphasis given to choosing and designing culturally appropriate strategies to investigate latino health, education, social context, and policy issues.

title: United States Political Institutions & Chicanas/os
description: Course Description: Overview of the major political institutions and ideologies of the United States and the Chicana/o people's historical and contemporary role in, effects from, and responses to them. Theory, method and critical analysis.

title: Comparative Health: Top Leading Causes of Death
description: Course Description: Introduction to the epidemiology of the leading causes of death for ethnic/racial minorities. Assessment of disproportionate rates at which ethnic/racial minorities suffer and die from chronic and infectious diseases and injuries and statistical methods used to calculate these rates.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): STA 013 or STA 013Y; or consent of instructor.

title: Comparative Health: Leading Causes of Death
description: Course Description: Introduction to epidemiology of leading causes of death for ethnic/racial minorities. Assessment of disproportionate rates at which ethnic/racial minorities suffer & die from chronic and infectious diseases & injuries & statistical methods used to calculate these rates.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): STA 013 or STA 013Y; or consent of instructor.

title: Food Justice: Chicana/o & Indigenous Communities
description: Course Description: Issues surrounding food justice in Chicana/o and Indigenous Communities. Emphasis on discourses and practices of growing a food justice movement centered on the ecological care of the earth and decolonized environmental methodologies.

title: Chicana & Chicano Culture
description: Course Description: Interdisciplinary survey of Chicana/o cultural representation in the 20th century. Examines Chicana/o culture within a national and transnational context. Explores how Chicano cultural forms and practices intersect with social/material forces, intellectual formations and cultural discourses. (Former CHI 020.)

title: Chicana & Chicano Representation in Cinema
description: Course Description: Introductory-level study of Chicana and Chicano representation in cinema. Depiction of Chicana and Chicano experience by Chicana/o filmmakers, as well as by non-Chicanos, including independent filmmakers and the commercial industry.

title: New Latin American Cinema
description: Course Description: Historical, critical, and theoretical survey of the cinemas of Latin America and their relationship to the emergence of U.S. Latino cinema. Emphasis on representation and social identity including gender, sexuality, class, race and ethnicity.

title: Survey of Chicana/o Art
description: Course Description: Survey of contemporary Chicana/o art in context of the social turmoil from which it springs. Includes political use of the poster and the mural, the influence of the Mexican mural and graphic movement, and social responsibility of the artist.

title: Chicana/o Art Expression Through Silk Screen
description: Course Description: Introductory level studio course using silk screen and basic printing techniques to explore and develop images of Chicana/o cultural themes and expressions. Students experiment with images and symbols from their immediate environment/culture. Integrated approach to Chicana/o philosophy of art.

title: Internship
description: This version has ended; see updated course, below.Course Description: Academic guidance combined with internship in community agencies serving Mexican/Latina/Latino/Chicana/Chicano clients. Students will use their bilingual skills and knowledge of history, culture, economics, politics and social issues.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): CHI 010; or consent of instructor.

title: Directed Group Study
description: Course Description: Directed group study.

title: Special Study for Undergraduates
description: Course Description: Special study for undergraduates.

title: Chicana/o Theoretical Perspective
description: This version has ended; see updated course, below.Course Description: Critical examination of emerging Chicana/o Studies theoretical perspectives in light of contemporary intellectual frameworks in the social sciences, arts, and humanities. Includes analysis of practices of self-representation, and socio-cultural developments in the Chicana/o community.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): CHI 010; CHI 050.

title: Chicana/o Feminist Theoretical Understandings of K-20 Educational Disparities
description: Course Description: Examination of educational disparities of the K-20 educational system. Chicana/o education theory and analysis with a specific emphasis on feminist frameworks and analytical tools used to guide and inform educational policy-making.

title: Grassroots Community Activism & Mobilization Efforts Challenging Educational Inequity
description: Course Description: Exploration and research on effective grassroots community activism and mobilization efforts by Chicana/o students, along with their teachers, families, and other allies to protest structured inequality of the U.S. educational system. Mentoring and tutoring in a school under the supervision of a faculty member is required.

title: Policy & Law Challenging Segregation & Educational Inequity
description: Course Description: Focus on successful lawsuits against school segregation of Mexican-origin children in the United States. Mentoring and tutoring in a school under the supervision of a faculty member is required.

title: Sociology of the Chicana/o Experience
description: This version has ended; see updated course, below.Course Description: The Chicana/o experience in the American society and economy viewed from theoretical perspectives. Immigration, history of integration of Chicana/o labor into American class structure, education inequality, ethnicity, the family and Chicana/o politics. (Former course SOC 110.)
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): CHI 010 or SOC 001 or SOC 001V.

title: Sociology of the Chicana/o Experience
description: This version has ended; see updated course, below.Course Description: Chicana/o experience in the American society and economy viewed from theoretical perspectives. Immigration, history of integration of Chicana/o labor into American class structure, education inequality, ethnicity, the family and Chicana/o politics. (Former course SOC 110)
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): CHI 010 or SOC 001 or SOC 001V.

title: Chicanas/Mexicanas in Contemporary Society
description: This version has ended; see updated course, below.Course Description: Analysis of the role and status of Chicanas/Mexicanas in contemporary society. Special emphasis on their historical role, the political, economic and social institutions that have affected their status, and their contributions to society and their community. (Former CHI 102.)
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): CHI 010 or CHI 050; (WMS 050 or HIS 169B).

title: Globalization, Transnational Migration, & Chicana/o & Latina/o Communities
description: This version has ended; see updated course, below.Course Description: Chicana/o and Latina/o migration experiences within a global context. Topics include national and/or transnational migration in Mexico, Central America, and the United States.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): CHI 010.

title: Latin American Women’s Engagement in Social Movements
description: This version has ended; see updated course, below.Course Description: Examination of how women of different racial/ethnic and class backgrounds in Latin America challenge their marginalization. Exploration of US foreign policy, its effects on Latin American’s institutions and on Latin American citizens. Using Chicana feminist perspective.

title: Latin American Women’s Engagement in Social Movements
description: Course Description: Examination of how women of different racial/ethnic and class backgrounds in Latin America challenge their marginalization. Exploration of US foreign policy, its effects on Latin American’s institutions and on Latin American citizens. Using Chicana feminist perspective.

title: Women of Color Reproductive Health & Reproductive Politics in a Global Perspective
description: This version has ended; see updated course, below.Course Description: Study contemporary issues in reproductive health and reproductive politics, both globally and in the U.S., for women of color.

title: Women of Color Reproductive Health & Gender Politics in Cuba & the U.S.
description: Course Description: Study of contemporary issues in reproductive health, reproductive politics, and gender politics both in Cuba and in the U.S., for women of color.

title: Women of Color Reproductive Health & Reproductive Politics in a Global Perspective
description: Course Description: Study contemporary issues in reproductive health and reproductive politics, both globally and in the U.S., for women of color.

title: Central Americans in the U.S.: Legacies, Identities, & Social Movements
description: Course Description: Central Americans in the United States, United States intervention and the consequent diaspora. Identity formations, health status, academic achievement of unaccompanied minors, and refugees are considered using intersectional analysis and with emphasis on how they are shaped by immigration policies.

title: Chicana/o Psychology
description: Course Description: Introduction to the field of Chicana/o psychology. Analysis of socio-cultural context of Chicanas/os and Latinas/os. Special attention to issues of ethnic identity development, bilingualism, and development of self esteem. Impact of minority experience, migration, acculturation are examined.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): CHI 021; Introductory psychology course recommended.

title: Chicana/o Community Mental Health
description: This version has ended; see updated course, below.Course Description: Mental health needs, problems, and service utilization patterns of Chicanas/os and Latinas/os will be analyzed. Analysis of social service policy, and the economic context of mental health programs.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): CHI 010; or CHI 020.

title: Psychology Perspectives Chicana/o & Latina/o Family
description: This version has ended; see updated course, below.Course Description: Role of migration and acculturation on family structure and functioning. From a psychological and Chicana/o Studies perspective, contemporary gender roles and variations in family structures are examined. Special topics include family violence, addiction, family resilience and coping strategies.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): CHI 010; and consent of instructor; introductory psychology course highly recommended.

title: Psychology Perspectives Chicana/o & Latina/o Family
description: Course Description: Role of migration and acculturation on family structure and functioning. From a psychological and Chicana/o Studies perspective, contemporary gender roles and variations in family structures are examined. Special topics include family violence, addiction, family resilience and coping strategies.

title: Psychological perspectives on Chicana/o & Latina/o Children & Adolescents
description: This version has ended; see updated course, below.Course Description: Psychological and educational development of Chicano/Latino children and adolescents, with particular attention to the formation of ethnic, gender, class, race, and sexual identities.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): CHI 010 or CHI 021.

title: Latino Families in the Age of Globalization: Migration & Transculturation
description: Course Description: Impact of globalization on Latino families in the American continent. Relationships of political structure, economics and family. Intimate partner violence, child maltreatment and alcohol/drug abuse in contemporary Latino families.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): SPA 003 or the equivalent highly recommended.

title: United States-Mexican Border Relations
description: Course Description: Theories of U.S.-Mexican border relations, with an overview of the political, economic, and social relationships and an in-depth analysis of immigration issues, border industrialization, women's organizations, economic crises, and legal issues.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Upper division standing.

title: Chicanas in Politics & Public Policy
description: Course Description: Historical and political analysis of Chicana/Latina political involvement and activities in the general political system, women's movement, Chicano movement, and Chicana movement. Examines the public policy process and the relationship of Chicanas/Latinas to public policy formation.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): CHI 030 or POL 001 or POL 001Y.

title: Chicanas in Politics & Public Policy
description: Course Description: Historical and political analysis of Chicana/Latina political involvement and activities in the general political system, women's movement, Chicano/a movement. Examines the public policy process and the relationship of Chicanas/Latinas to public policy formation.

title: Political Economy of Chicana/o Communities
description: Course Description: Historical and contemporary study of political and economic forces which define and influence the development of Chicana/o communities. Includes critiques of traditional and Marxian theories and concepts applicable to Chicana/o communities, case studies of Chicana/o communities,especially in California and Texas.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Upper division standing; lower division Chicana/o Studies (CHI) course recommended.

title: Transnational Latina/o Political Economy
description: Course Description: Intensive reading, discussion and research on selected topics from Latin America and the U.S. with regard to immigrant and native communities. Topics include comparative immigration and macroeconomic policies in the U.S. and Latin America.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): SPA 003 or SPA 003V or SPA 003Y; or consent of instructor, or equivalent; ECN 001A and ECN 001B recommended.

title: Critical & Abolitionist Pedagogies
description: Course Description: How marginalized groups conceptualize learning and teaching as a practice of freedom. Foundational text and concepts in critical pedagogy. Diverse and intersecting intellectual traditions that inform how oppressed peoples use education as a tool of liberation.

title: Quantitative Methods: Chicano/Latino Health Research
description: Course Description: Focuses on measuring Latino/Chicano health outcomes using a quantitative approach. Assesses main types of study designs and addresses measurement of disease frequency and health effects.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Two years of high school algebra or the equivalent in college.

title: Community-Based Participatory Research & Chicana/o & Latina/o Health
description: This version has ended; see updated course, below.Course Description: Overview of CBPR, as well as methodological CBPR considerations in building community partnerships, community assessment, issue analysis, research planning, data gathering, and data sharing with Chicana/o and Latina/o communities in particular.

title: Community-Based Participatory Research & Chicana/o & Latina/o Health
description: Course Description: Overview of CBPR, as well as methodological CBPR considerations in building community partnerships, community assessment, issue analysis, research planning, data gathering, and data sharing with Chicana/o and Latina/o communities in particular.

title: Bi-National Health
description: Course Description: Examination of health status and intervention strategies presented in public health care settings, private clinics and by indigenous healers in Mexico. Analysis of impact of high risk diseases. Offered in a Spanish speaking country under supervision of UC Davis faculty/lecturer; may be taught abroad.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): BIS 001A; BIS 001B; BIS 001C; (SPA 021 or SPA 021V or SPA 021Y or SPA 031); or consent of instructor. Upper division standing only.

title: Public Health in Latin America
description: Course Description: Critical examination of emerging Public Health issues in Latin America in light of economic, political and social conditions. Contemporary behavioral frameworks used in public health. Includes analysis of clinical medicine and health care systems.

title: Indigenous Healing & Biodiversity in Latin America
description: Course Description: Contrast between western and traditional healing practices in Latin America and the role of the natural environment in creating sustainable health delivery systems. Questions of health status attributable to public health and environmental risk factors. May be taught abroad.

title: Decolonizing Spirit
description: Course Description: Legacies of colonization and decolonization; indigenous forms of spirituality and sacredness. Emphasis on remembering traditions, practices, relations, and forms of indigenous knowledge.

title: The Chicana & Chicano Movement
description: Course Description: Development of the Chicano Movement within the context of the socio-political movements of the 1960’s in a national and global perspective. Ideological/political perspectives and the implications for political strategies.

title: The Chicana/o Novel
description: Course Description: Introduction to the forms and themes of the Chicana/o novel with special attention to the construction of gender, nationality, sexuality, social class, and the family by contemporary Chicana/o novelists. Bilingual readings, lectures, discussions, and writing in Spanish. (Former course SPA 126A.)
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Intermediate Spanish or consent of instructor.

title: Chicana/o Theater
description: Course Description: Examination of the formal and thematic dimensions of Chicana/o theater in the contemporary period with special emphasis on El Teatro Campesino and Chicana Feminist Theater. Bilingual readings, lectures, discussions, and writing in Spanish. (Former course SPA 126B.)
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Intermediate Spanish or consent of instructor.

title: Chicana/o Poetry
description: Course Description: Survey of Chicana/o poetry with special emphasis on its thematic and formal dimensions. Bilingual readings, lectures, discussions, and writing in Spanish. (Former course SPA 126C.)
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Intermediate Spanish or consent of instructor.

title: Chicana & Chicano Narrative
description: Course Description: Exploration of contemporary forms of the Chicana and Chicano narrative, encompassing visual art, fiction, poetry, film, theater, and creative nonfiction. Exposure to a variety of artists and scholars whose work shapes our evolving understanding of the Chicana/o experience.

title: Contemporary Issues in Latinx Literature
description: Course Description: Contemporary Chicanx Literature within the broader umbrella of Latinx Literature. Genres, including fiction, nonfiction, and poetry, but also hybrid and mixed genre texts. New trends and topics relating to 21st Century Latinx authors, including intersectionality and multiple identities, AfroLatinx and Indigenous identity, Queer representation, Young Adult literature, and undocumented writers.

title: Mexican Film & Greater Mexican Identity
description: Course Description: Survey of the role Mexican cinema plays in consolidation and contestation of post-revoluationary Mexican state and in the formation of a Greater Mexican cultural identity including Chicana/o identity. Showcases genres, perios, auteurs, movements and emphasis on gendered and sexualized narratives.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Intermediate Spanish.

title: Queer Latinidad
description: Course Description: Introduction to queer Latina and Latino studies with a focus on Chicana and Chicano theory and cultural production.

title: Chicanas, Latinas & Mexicanas in Commercial Media
description: Course Description: The portrayal of Chicanas, Latinas and Mexicanas in commercial media. The relation between the representation of Chicana, Latina, and Mexicana women in commercial television and cinema and the role of women in Mexican and U.S. societies.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): CHI 060; or other film or feminist theory course; conversational fluency in Spanish.

title: Contemporary Issues in Chicano Art
description: Course Description: Issues and conflicts in the dismantling of the Contemporary Chicano Art Movement. Response and challenge to the dominant culture.

title: Mexican & Chicano Mural Workshop
description: Course Description: The Mural: a collective art process that empowers students and people through design and execution of mural paintings in the tradition of the Mexican Mural Movement; introduces materials and techniques.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): CHI 070; and consent of instructor.

title: Chicana/o Voice/Poster Silk Screen Workshop
description: Course Description: The poster as a voice art form used by Chicanas/os and other people of color to point to the defects of social and political existence and the possibility for change, from the Chicana/o artists' perspective.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): CHI 070 or CHI 073; and consent of instructor.

title: Grant Writing in the Chicana/o/Latina/o Community
description: This version has ended; see updated course, below.Course Description: Overview of key elements for grant writing. Topics include community needs assessments, development of human subjects protocols, data collection, methods, evaluation designs and community based methodologies for grant development applications in the Latino community.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): CHI 010 or CHI 023; or consent of instructor.

title: Chicanas & Latinas in the U.S.: Historical Perspectives
description: This version has ended; see updated course, below.Course Description: Historical issues in the lives of Chicanas and Latinas in the U.S. and their diverse countries of origin.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): CHI 010 or WMS 050.

title: Race & Juvenile Justice
description: This version has ended; see updated course, below.Course Description: Individual and institutinal respnses to "troublesome" youth of color through history and in contemporary siciety. Emphasis on how race, as well as ethnicity, class, and gender have informed the treatment of "delinquent" youth.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): CHI 010; or equivalent.

title: Latino Youth Gangs in Global Perspective
description: Course Description: Comparative analysis of Latino youth gangs in Europe, Latin America, and the United States. Social, economic, political, and cultural factors leading to youth gangs as well as the responses are considered within a global perspective.

title: Latino Youth Gangs in Global Perspective
description: Course Description: Comparative analysis of Latino youth gangs in Europe, Latin America, and the United States. Social, economic, political, and cultural factors leading to youth gangs as well as the responses to the youths are considered within a global perspective.

title: Transforming Violence: Healing our Relations
description: This version has ended; see updated course, below.Course Description: Transforming violence with a focus on emerging abolitionist movements in community accountability and transformative justice; emphasis on the study and practice of nurturing life-honoring and care-centered sacred relations through decolonizing, indigenous, and spirit-centered traditions and cosmologies.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): CHI 010 (can be concurrent).

title: Internship in the Chicana/Chicano/Latina/Latino Community
description: This version has ended; see updated course, below.Course Description: Academic guidance combined with internship in community agencies serving Mexican/Latina/Latino/Chicana/Chicano clients. Use of bilingual skills and knowledge of history, culture, economics, politics and social issues. Internship project required.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): (CHI 010 or CHI 021 or CHI 050); (SPA 003 or SPA 003V or SPA 003Y); or equivalent of SPA 003.

title: Internship
description: This version has ended; see updated course, below.Course Description: Internship. May be taught abroad.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): (CHI 010 or CHI 021 or CHI 050); (SPA 003 or SPA 003V or SPA 003Y); and consent of instructor, or equivalent of SPA 003, SPA 003V, SPA 003Y.

title: Senior Honors Research Project
description: Course Description: Student is required to read, research, and write Honors Thesis on Chicana/o Studies topics.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Consent of instructor; senior standing in Chicana/o Studies major.

title: Senior Honors Research Project
description: Course Description: Student is required to read, research, and write Honors Thesis on Chicana/o Studies topics.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Consent of instructor; senior standing in Chicana/o Studies major.

title: Senior Honors Research Project
description: Course Description: Student is required to read, research, and write Honors Thesis on Chicana/o Studies topics.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Consent of instructor; senior standing in Chicana/o Studies major.

title: Directed Group Study
description: Course Description: Directed group study. May be taught abroad.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Upper division standing and consent of Program Chairperson.

title: Directed Group Study
description: Course Description: Directed group study.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Consent of instructor.

title: Special Study for Advanced Undergraduates
description: Course Description: Special study for advanced undergraduates.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Upper division standing and consent of Program Chairperson.

title: Special Study for Advanced Undergraduates
description: Course Description: Special study for advanced undergraduates.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Consent of instructor.

title: Chicano/Latino Hispanic Politics
description: Course Description: Examination of Chicano/Latino political experiences. Evaluate theories, ideology, and practice of Chicano politics. Brief history of Chicano/Latino/Hispanic political activity, comparisons among political modes, gendered politics, and understanding relationships among Chicano, Mexican, American and world politics.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Two undergraduate courses in Chicana/o Studies (CHI) or consent of instructor.

title: Community Based Participatory Research
description: Course Description: Provides knowledge & skills to plan and implement participatory & community based projects that highlight community empowerment framework.

title: Group Study for Graduate Students
description: Course Description: Group study for graduate students.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Consent of instructor; graduate standing.

title: Special Study for Graduate Students
description: Course Description: Special study for graduate students.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Consent of instructor; graduate standing.

title: Teaching Assistant Training Practicum
description: Course Description: Teaching assistant training practicum.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Consent of instructor; graduate standing.

title: Accelerated Written Chinese I
description: Course Description: Trainings on all the communicative skills of listening, speaking, reading, and writing for students who already have elementary level ability to understand or speak Mandarin Chinese. Emphases on standard Mandarin pronunciation, Chinese characters, and discourse level conversations.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Placement exam or consent of instructor.

title: Mandarin for Cantonese Speakers I
description: Course Description: Training in spoken Mandarin, particularly in the phonetic transcription system known as pinyin, for students who already read and write Chinese.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Placement exam or consent of instructor.

title: Elementary Chinese
description: Course Description: Continuation of elementary level skill development in listening, speaking, reading and writing Mandarin Chinese in everyday communication settings. Continued introduction of basic vocabulary and characters as well as core grammar, and further train pronunciation.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): CHN 001; or placement exam or consent of instructor.

title: Accelerated Written Chinese II
description: Course Description: Further trainings on all the communicative skills of listening, speaking, reading, and writing for students that already have elementary level ability to understand or speak Mandarin Chinese. Emphases on standard Mandarin pronunciation, Chinese characters, and discourse level conversations.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): CHN 001BL; or placement exam or consent of instructor.

title: Mandarin for Cantonese Speakers II
description: Course Description: Continuation of CHN 001CN. Training in spoken Mandarin for students who can already read and write Chinese.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): CHN 001CN; or placement exam or consent of instructor.

title: Elementary Chinese
description: Course Description: Continuation of elementary level skill development in listening, speaking, reading and writing Mandarin Chinese in everyday communication settings. Continued introduction of basic vocabulary and characters as well as core grammar, and further train pronunciation.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): CHN 002; or placement exam or consent of instructor.

title: Accelerated Written Chinese III
description: Course Description: Continuation of CHN 002BL with further trainings on all the communicative skills of listening, speaking, reading, and writing with emphases on standard Mandarin pronunciation, Chinese characters, and discourse level conversations in more communication settings.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): CHN 002BL; or placement exam or consent of instructor.

title: Mandarin for Cantonese Speakers III
description: Course Description: Continuation of CHN 002CN. Prepares students for entering upper division courses in Chinese.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): CHN 002CN; or placement exam or consent of instructor.

title: Intermediate Chinese
description: Course Description: Continuation of intermediate-level communication skills in spoken and written Mandarin, based on language skills developed in CHN 003.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): CHN 003 or placement exam or consent of instructor.

title: Accelerated Intensive Intermediate Chinese
description: Course Description: Special nine-week accelerated, intensive summer session course that combines the work of CHN 004, CHN 005, and CHN 006. Intermediate-level training in spoken and written Chinese in cultural and communicative contexts, based on language skills developed in CHN 003 or CHN 001A.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): CHN 003 or CHN 001A or placement exam or consent of instructor.

title: Intermediate Chinese
description: Course Description: Training continues at intermediate-level in spoken and written Chinese in cultural contexts, based on language skills developed in CHN 004.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): CHN 004 or placement exam or consent of instructor.

title: Intermediate Chinese
description: Course Description: Intermediate-level training in spoken and written Chinese in cultural contexts, based on language skills developed in CHN 005.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): CHN 005; CHN 005 or placement exam or consent of instructor.

title: Chinese Business Culture
description: Course Description: Introduction to business culture of China. Basic conversation and Romanization of Chinese words.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Consent of instructor.

title: Introduction to Chinese Language & Culture
description: Course Description: Chinese culture through the lens of Chinese language. Relationship of language to religion, food, arts, ethnic groups, dialects, writing systems, etc. All readings are in English; no previous background is necessary.

title: Modern Chinese Literature (In English)
description: Course Description: Introductory course requiring no knowledge of Chinese language or history. Reading and discussion of short stories and novels and viewing of two films. Designed to convey a feeling for what China has experienced in the 20th century.

title: Great Books of China (in English)
description: Course Description: Readings in English translation of classical and medieval Chinese poetry, Chinese historiography, tales of the supernatural, Tang romances, opera, and late imperial era vernacular literature, with key works by Sima Qian, Gan Bao, Tao Qian, Han Yu, and Feng Menglong. Background information on periods, authors and the interrelationships of culture, literature and social change.

title: Introduction to the Literature of China & Japan
description: Course Description: Methods of literary analysis and their application to major works from the various genres of Chinese and Japanese literature (in translation), including film. East Asian cultural traditions will also be introduced.

title: Special Study for Undergraduates
description: Course Description: Special study for undergraduates.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Consent of instructor.

title: Daoist Traditions
description: Course Description: English-language survey of key Daoist texts and scholarship. Topics include Daoist concepts of the cosmos, the natural world, scripture, the body, and immortality; Daoist divinities; Daoism and the state.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): A course in Chinese history recommended.

title: Confucian Traditions
description: Course Description: Key aspects of the Confucian tradition in dynastic China. Major themes addressed include ritual, classical studies, and Confucian influences on the Chinese family and state.

title: Chinese Film
description: Course Description: English language survey of Chinese film, from its inception to the end of the 20th century. Chinese films as important texts for understanding national, transnational, racial, gender, and class politics of modern China.

title: Modern Chinese Drama
description: Course Description: English language survey of modern Chinese spoken drama in the 20th century and its major playwrights, in the context of Chinese history and the interaction of Chinese culture with other cultures.

title: Modern Chinese Fiction (in English)
description: Course Description: English language survey of Chinese fiction as it evolved amidst the great historical, social and cultural changes of the 20th century. Thorough study of the most influential writers and genres.

title: Western Influences on 20th-Century Chinese Literature (in English)
description: Course Description: Introduction of Western literary thought into modern China, the experimentation with Western literary forms and techniques, and the development of Marxism in contemporary literary writing.

title: Chinese Poetry (in English)
description: Course Description: Organized topically and chronologically, the lyric tradition is explored from the dawn of folk songs down to modern expressions of social protest. Topics include friendship, love, oppression, war, parting, death, ecstasy and beauty. All readings are in English.

title: Traditional Chinese Fiction (in English)
description: Course Description: Dawn of Chinese fiction and its development down to modern times. Survey of literary history with close reading of representative works. Readings, lectures, and discussions in English.

title: Poetry of China & Japan (in English)
description: Course Description: A comparative approach to Chinese and Japanese poetry, examining poetic practice in the two cultures; includes a general outline of the two traditions, plus study of poetic forms, techniques, and distinct treatments of universal themes: love, nature, war, etc.

title: Topics in Chinese Literature: Crime & Punishment
description: Course Description: Examines how crime and punishment have been represented in traditional Chinese short stories, novels, operas, and histories.

title: Topics in Chinese Literature: Women Writers (in English)
description: Course Description: Topics in Chinese literature; women writers.

title: Topics in Chinese Literature: The Knight-Errant (in English)
description: Course Description: Topics in Chinese literature; the knight-errant.

title: Topics in Chinese Literature: The City in Fiction (in English)
description: Course Description: Topics in Chinese literature; the city in fiction.

title: Topics in Chinese Literature: The Literature of 20th-Century Taiwan (in English)
description: Course Description: Topics in Chinese literature; the literature of 20th-century Taiwan.

title: Topics in Chinese Literature: Popular Literature (in English)
description: Course Description: Topics in Chinese literature; popular literature.

title: Topics in Chinese Literature: Scholar & The Courtesan (in English)
description: Course Description: Chinese romance stories, focusing on depictions of scholars and courtesans in Tang fables, late imperial operas, Ming short-stories, and 19th-century novels.

title: Great Writers of China: Texts & Context (in English)
description: Course Description: Examination of major theoretical concepts and interpretive methods in the study of literature by using examples from the Chinese tradition; discussions of classical and modern works with an emphasis on the relations between literature, author, society, and culture.

title: Modern Chinese: Reading & Discussion
description: Course Description: Building on Chinese 006/003BL, further development of communication skills in Modern Standard Mandarin-speaking environments. Reading of dialogues/articles pertaining to contemporary China.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): CHN 006 C- or better or CHN 003BL C- or better or CHN 004A C- or better; or placement exam or consent of instructor.

title: Intensive Third-Year Chinese
description: Course Description: Nine-week intensive summer course combines CHN 111, CHN 112, and CHN 113. Training at intermediate-high and advanced-low level in spoken and written Chinese in cultural and communicative contexts based on language skills developed in CHN 006.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): CHN 006 or CHN 003BL or CHN 004A; or placement exam or consent of instructor.

title: Modern Chinese: Reading & Discussion
description: Course Description: Further development of communication skills from CHN 111 in Modern Standard Mandarin-speaking environments. Reading dialogues/articles pertaining to contemporary China issues and discussing ethical, moral, aesthetic, social, and cultural concerns.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): CHN 111; or placement exam or consent of instructor.

title: Modern Chinese: Reading & Discussion
description: Course Description: Continuation of CHN 112, further developing communication skills in Modern Standard Mandarin-speaking environments. Read dialogues/articles pertaining to contemporary China issues and discuss ethical, moral, aesthetic, social, and cultural concerns. Study strategies for moving between simplified and traditional Chinese characters.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): CHN 112; or placement exam or consent of instructor.

title: Introduction to Classical Chinese
description: Course Description: Introduction to the language in which, until the 20th century, most official, documentary, scholarly, and belle-lettristic Chinese literature was written.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): CHN 112; or equivalent language proficiency. consent of instructor.

title: Introduction to Classical Chinese II
description: Course Description: Continuation of enhancing classical Chinese reading skills with literature ranging from the prose found in Han dynasty historical works, Six Dynasties anecdotal literature, and Tang occasional texts, as well as the poetic shi and fu genres.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): CHN 114; or Consent of instructor.

title: Introduction to Classical Chinese III
description: Course Description: Translations of extended readings in the original sources and brief analyses of syntax. These sources will include texts written by well-known figures from the 8th through 15th centuries, composing in a wide variety of genres.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): CHN 115; or consent of instructor.

title: Advanced Chinese for Non-Native Speakers
description: Course Description: Analysis of material from various genres (literature, newspapers, blogs, TV, film, etc) with focus on Chinese society and culture. Advanced reading, writing, aural comprehension, and formal speech in Mandarin Chinese. For students who have completed the third-year Chinese series (CHN 111-113), but do not have substantial background as heritage or native speakers of Chinese.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): CHN 113; or equivalent language proficiency; consent of instructor.

title: Advanced Chinese
description: Course Description: Evaluation of readings from various genres (literature, newspapers, TV and movies, etc.) develop advanced reading, writing, aural comprehension, and formal/professional speech skills in Mandarin Chinese. Chinese society/cultural studies, especially those sociocultural issues reflected in the language used in learning materials.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): CHN 113; or placement exam or consent of instructor.

title: Business Chinese
description: Course Description: Mandarin Chinese language for business and professional purposes. Appropriate usages and protocols within the context of Chinese business culture.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): CHN 113 or consent of instructor.

title: Readings in Traditional Chinese Fiction
description: Course Description: Examination of representative works of traditional Chinese fiction popular from the 12th Century until the 17th and 18th centuries. Translations in English of the Chinese texts will be available to students as reference.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): CHN 111; or equivalent language proficiency.

title: Readings in Traditional Chinese Poetry
description: Course Description: Traditional Chinese poetry from its beginnings to the golden ages of Tang and Song, surveying forms and poets that best reveal the Chinese poetic sensibility and the genius of the language of Chinese poetry.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): CHN 111; consent of instructor, or equivalent language proficiency.

title: Readings in Modern Chinese Poetry
description: Course Description: Chinese poetry from the Literary Revolution of 1917 to the present, surveying works that embody exciting innovations and reflect the modernity of 20th-century Chinese society and culture.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): CHN 111; consent of instructor, or equivalent language proficiency.

title: Readings in Modern Chinese Prose & Drama
description: Course Description: Literary works and scholarly essays on selected topics of Chinese prose and drama, development of a deep understanding of Chinese culture and society through sophisticated reading materials of these two important genres of the modern period.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): CHN 111; or equivalent language proficiency, or consent of instructor.

title: Chinese Film in Chinese Language
description: Course Description: Chinese film and scholarly essays on Chinese cinema and film history. Develop a deep understanding of Chinese culture and society through viewing and studying Chinese films in the Chinese language.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): CHN 111; or equivalent language proficiency.

title: Readings in Classical Chinese
description: Course Description: Study and philological analysis of selected texts from the first millennium of Imperial China.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Consent of instructor.

title: Fifth-Year Chinese: Selected Topics in Chinese Language, Literature, & Culture
description: Course Description: Examination of literary works and scholarly essays on selected topics of Chinese culture and society. Development of a deep understanding of Chinese culture and society through sophisticated Chinese speaking and writing exercises.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): CHN 120; or placement exam or consent of instructor.

title: The Chinese Language
description: Course Description: Introduction to structural features of Chinese (Mandarin) sounds, lexicon, grammar, and writing (characters), as well as relevant dialectal and sociolinguistic issues of the language.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): CHN 006 (can be concurrent) or CHN 003BL (can be concurrent) or CHN 003CN (can be concurrent) or CHN 004A (can be concurrent); or placement exam or consent of instructor; LIN 001 recommended.

title: Chinese Internship
description: Course Description: Work experience in the Chinese language, with analytical term paper on a topic approved by instructor.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Upper division standing and consent of instructor.

title: Senior Thesis Honors Project
description: Course Description: Guided research, under the direction of a faculty member, leading to a senior honors thesis on a topic in Chinese literature, civilization, or language studies.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Consent of instructor; senior standing and qualification for the Chinese honors program.

title: Tutoring in Chinese
description: Course Description: Leading of small voluntary discussion groups affiliated with one of the Department's regular courses.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Consent of Department.

title: Directed Group Study
description: Course Description: Directed group study.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Consent of instructor.

title: Special Study for Advanced Undergraduates
description: Course Description: Special study for advanced undergraduates.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Consent of instructor.

title: Directed Independent Study
description: Course Description: Directed independent study on a topic culminating in a term paper. Independent studies may only be arranged with consent of the instructor and when graduate seminars are unavailable.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Consent of instructor.

title: Research
description: Course Description: Research.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Consent of instructor.

title: Teaching Assistant Training Practicum
description: Course Description: Any course taught by a graduate student under the direction of the Director.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Consent of instructor; graduate standing.

title: Ancient Near East & Early Greece: 3000-500 B.C.E.
description: Course Description: Introduction to the literature, art, and social and political institutions of ancient Mesopotamia, Egypt, Palestine, and early Greece from 3000 to 500 B.C.E.

title: Ancient Greece & the Near East: 500-146 B.C.E.
description: Course Description: Introduction to the literature, art and thought and the political and social institutions and values of Greece and its eastern Mediterranean neighbors-the Persians, Egyptians, and Judeans.

title: Rome & the Mediterranean: 800 B.C.E.-500 C.E.
description: Course Description: Introduction to the history, literature, material culture, political and social institutions and values of Roman Civilization, with an emphasis on the development of the Roman Empire and the interactions of Roman culture with other Mediterranean cultures.

title: Late Antiquity
description: Course Description: History and culture of the Roman and Byzantine empires from the 3rd to the 8th century. Transformation of the classical Mediterranean world through political and cultural interactions, rise of Christianity and Islam, beginning of the medieval period in Europe.

title: World Classical Cultures
description: Course Description: Study of selected ancient cultures of the world. Analysis of the construction of terms such as “classics,” “civilization,” and “culture.” Examination of the plural legacies of classical cultures in the modern world. Ancient cultures examined may include the Greco-Roman Mediterranean, Egypt, Maya Mesoamerica, Han China, Maurya South Asia, Mesopotamia.

title: Greek, Roman, & Near Eastern Mythology
description: Course Description: Examination of major myths of Greece, Rome, and the Ancient Near East; their place in the religion, literature and art of the societies that produced them; their subsequent development, influence and interpretation.

title: Greek, Roman, & Near Eastern Mythology–Hybrid
description: Course Description: Examination of major myths of Greece, Rome, and the Ancient Near East; their place in the religion, literature and art of the societies that produced them; their subsequent development, influence and interpretation.

title: Women & Gender in Classical Antiquity
description: Course Description: Lives and roles of women and men in ancient Greece and Rome. Readings from history, philosophy, medical and legal documents, literature and myth.

title: Pompeii AD 79
description: Course Description: Roman life in an urban community at the time of the eruption of Vesuvius. Slide presentations of the archeological evidence will be supplemented by selected readings from Petronius' Satyricon and other ancient authors.

title: The Classical Heritage in America
description: Course Description: Classical heritage in the New World, with emphasis on the United States from its colonial past to the present day. The reception of Greco-Roman thought and values as expressed in art, architecture, education, law, government, literature, and film.

title: Greek & Latin Elements in English Vocabulary
description: Course Description: Knowledge of Latin and Greek not required. Elements of Greek and Latin vocabulary for increased understanding of English word formation and improved ability to understand and retain unfamiliar words. Emphasis on Greek and Latin elements but other languages not neglected.

title: Greek & Latin Elements in English Vocabulary
description: Course Description: Knowledge of Latin and Greek not required. Elements of Greek and Latin vocabulary for increased understanding of English word formation and improved ability to understand and retain unfamiliar words. Emphasis on Greek and Latin elements but other languages not neglected.

title: Greek & Latin Elements in English Vocabulary
description: Course Description: Knowledge of Latin and Greek not required. Elements of Greek and Latin vocabulary for increased understanding of English word formation and improved ability to understand and retain unfamiliar words. Emphasis on Greek and Latin elements but other languages not neglected.

title: Greek & Latin Elements in English Vocabulary
description: Course Description: Knowledge of Latin and Greek not required. Elements of Greek and Latin vocabulary for increased understanding of English word formation and improved ability to understand and retain unfamiliar words. Emphasis on Greek and Latin elements but other languages not neglected.

title: Greek & Latin Elements in Technical Vocabulary
description: Course Description: Knowledge of Greek and Latin not required. Elements of Greek and Latin vocabulary to increase understanding of English word formation in medical, scientific and technical terminology and improve ability to understand and retain unfamiliar terms.

title: Food & Wine in the Ancient Mediterranean
description: Course Description: Social, political, and economic history of food and wine in ancient Mediterranean cultures. Development of agriculture and technology, trade, empires. Representation of food and wine in literary and visual arts, religious significance.

title: Food & Wine in Ancient Greece
description: Course Description: Social, political, and economic history of food and wine in Ancient Greece. Development of agriculture and technology, trade, cross-cultural exchanges. Representation of food and wine in literary and visual arts, religious significance. Online format combining asynchronous discussion with technologically based materials.

title: Life & Economy in the Ancient Mediterranean World
description: Course Description: Characterization of ancient Mediterranean economies, with emphasis on Greece and Rome. Utilization of archaeological, art historical, and literary evidence. Craft production, labor specialization, trade networks, ancient technology, urban growth, agricultural productivity, coinage systems, and household economies.

title: Sport & Spectacle in the Ancient Mediterranean
description: Course Description: Cultural practices and attitudes related to sport and spectacle in the ancient Mediterranean. Secular and ritual festivals and competitions, funerary games, gladiatorial combat and violent mass entertainment, royal hunts and imperial triumphs, and literary, artistic, and philosophical depictions of ancient athletes and physical performances and competitions.

title: Ancient Science
description: Course Description: Study of science in ancient Greece and Rome; consideration of its social context; concentration on the basic concepts of physics, the world of medicine and biology, the history of mathematics, and the practices of astronomy, astrology and meteorology.

title: Ancient Medicine
description: Course Description: Medicine in ancient Greece and Rome; physiological conceptions of the body within scientific and social frameworks; exploration of sanitation technology and health in antiquity; medical treatment of the female body; medicine and the economy.

title: Magic in the Ancient Greco-Roman World
description: Course Description: Magical practices and theories in ancient Greece and Rome and in the wider Mediterranean, including Mesopotamia and Egypt. Curses and spells in material culture, literary depictions of spell-casters, magic’s place in ancient society, and the modern afterlife of ancient magical practices.

title: Directed Group Study
description: Course Description: Directed group study.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Consent of instructor.

title: Methods for Ancient Mediterranean Studies
description: Course Description: Survey of methods and major scholarly schools in study of Ancient Mediterranean. Modern history of academic disciplines, archaeological work, influence of museums and other large institutions.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Lower division Classics course; or consent of instructor

title: Writing for Ancient Mediterranean Studies
description: Course Description: Advanced instruction in writing and practice in effective styles of communication for Ancient Mediterranean Studies and related academic and professional fields.

title: Topics in Ancient Mediterranean Civilizations
description: Course Description: Topics may be ordered by time or place (e.g. Hellenistic Egypt) or by theme or genre (e.g. slavery in the ancient world).
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): A lower division Classics (CLA) course or consent of instructor.

title: Topics in Greek Civilization
description: Course Description: Topics may be ordered by time or place (e.g. the world of Homer) or by theme or genre (e.g. the Greek art of war).
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): A lower division Classics (CLA) course or consent of instructor.

title: Topics in Roman Civilization
description: Course Description: Topics may be ordered by time or place (e.g. Julius Caesar and his age) or by theme or genre (e.g. gladiators: blood in the arena).
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): A lower division Classics (CLA) course or consent of instructor.

title: Topics in Classical Receptions
description: Course Description: Topics in classical reception from late antiquity to the present. Topics may be ordered by time or place (e.g. the classical tradition in Washington, D.C.) or by theme or genre (e.g. cinematic representations of the ancient world).
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): A lower division Classics (CLA) course or consent of instructor.

title: Topics in Ancient Science
description: Course Description: Topics may be ordered by discipline (e.g. ancient medicine), historical figure (e.g. Galen) or topic (e.g. science and the economy).
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): CLA 050 or CLA 051; or consent of instructor.

title: Film & the Classical World
description: Course Description: Classical World as portrayed in films. Viewings and discussions of modern versions of ancient dramas, modern dramas set in the Ancient Mediterranean world, and films imbued with classical themes and allusions. Supplementary readings in ancient literature and mythology.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): A lower division Classics (CLA) course or consent of instructor.

title: Love & Beauty in the Ancient World
description: Course Description: Philosophical and literary traditions connecting love, beauty, and goodness in ancient thought. Moral and ethical implications, ideologies of sexuality and gender; transmission into the medieval and modern world.

title: Death & Dying in the Ancient Mediterranean
description: Course Description: Ideas and practices related to death in the ancient Mediterranean. Burial rituals and spaces, philosophical and medical theories of death and the afterlife, the dying and dead in social life, and literary and artistic depictions of violence and the dead.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): One lower-divison Classics (CLA) course; or consent of instructor.

title: Theory & Practice of Greek & Roman Mythology
description: Course Description: Thematically focused study of mythological narratives. Emphasis on the historical development of myths and the variety of theoretical approaches for the study of myth.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): A lower division Classics (CLA) course or consent of instructor.

title: Latine/x & Latin American Perspectives & the Classical Tradition
description: Course Description: Latine/x and Latin American engagement with the classical tradition. Literary, cinematic, and artistic adaptations of Greco-Roman literature, art, and material culture. Social and political responses to the intellectual milieu of the ancient Mediterranean.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): One lower divison Classics (CLA) course; or consent of instructor.

title: Origins of Rhetoric
description: Course Description: Issues in the development of rhetoric from its origins in ancient Greece to A.D.430. Special attention to works of Plato, Aristotle, Cicero, and Quintilian. Role of grammar and rhetoric in schools of Roman Empire. The Christian rhetoric of Saint Augustine.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): A lower division Classics (CLA) course or consent of instructor.

title: Forms of Knowledge in the Ancient World
description: Course Description: History of knowledge preservation and transfer in the ancient Mediterranean. Oral tradition, technology, innovations, forms of writing, libraries, ancient scholarship, cultural exchange and influence.

title: Greek & Roman Historiography
description: Course Description: Survey of Greek and Roman historical writing in English translation. Authors to be read may include Herodotus, Thucydides, Sallust, Livy, and Tacitus. Focus on the development of historical writing as a literary genre.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): A lower division Classics (CLA) course or consent of instructor.

title: Roman Political Thought
description: Course Description: Survey of Roman thinking about politics, as expressed both in formal theorizing and in a variety of other contexts, including oratory, historiography, and epic. Study of Roman political reflection in its historical, cultural, and literary context.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): A lower division Classics (CLA) course or consent of instructor.

title: Homer & Ancient Epic
description: Course Description: Reading of the classical epics of Homer (Iliad, Odyssey) and Virgil (Aeneid) in English. Discussion of techniques of composition, the beliefs and values of their respective societies, and the generic tradition of ancient epic.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): A lower division Classics (CLA) course or consent of instructor.

title: Greek & Roman Comedy
description: Course Description: Readings in Aristophanes, Menander, Plautus, and Terence; lectures on the development of ancient comedy.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): A lower division Classics (CLA) course or consent of instructor.

title: Greek & Roman Novel
description: Course Description: Examination of the ancient Greek romances and their development into the grotesque realism of Petronius' Satyricon, and the religious mysticism of Apuleius' The Golden Ass.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): A lower division Classics (CLA) course or consent of instructor.

title: Greek Tragedy
description: Course Description: Reading in English of selected plays of Aeschylus, Sophocles, and Euripides. Discussion of the development and influence of Athenian tragedy.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): A lower division Classics (CLA) course or consent of instructor.

title: Socrates & Classical Athens
description: Course Description: Study of the major sources of our knowledge of Socrates, assessment of his role in the politics and culture of ancient Athens, his method of teaching, and his place in Western thought.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): A lower division Classics (CLA) course or consent of instructor.

title: Age of Nero
description: Course Description: Roman history, culture, and society of the 1st century CE under the reign of the emperor Nero. Nero's own imperial project and lasting legacy, major artistic and literary contributions under his reign, and how the Neronian period fits into the larger narrative of ancient Rome.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): A lower division Classics course or consent of instructor.

title: Roman Epic
description: Course Description: Reading in English of selected Roman epic poems. Authors may include Lucretius, Vergil, Ovid, Lucan, Silius Italicus, Valerius Flaccus, and/or Statius. Relationship between the Greek and Roman epic tradition, the political and social contexts of the works under discussion, and the critical and creative reception of Roman epic.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): A lower division Classics course or consent of instructor.

title: Cultural Interactions in the Ancient Mediterranean World
description: Course Description: Exploration of the role of colonial encounters in the spread of ideas throughout the ancient Mediterranean from an archaeological and artistic perspective. Emphasis on material and literary expressions of culture, trade routes, and theories pertaining to culture contact.

title: Mediterranean Bronze Age Archaeology
description: Course Description: Archaeological monuments of the ancient Near East, including Egypt and Mesopotamia, and of Greece and Crete during the Bronze Age. Special emphasis on the problems of state formation and on the co-existence and collapse of Bronze Age societies.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): A lower division Classics (CLA) course or consent of instructor.

title: Early Greek Art & Architecture
description: Course Description: Examination of the origin and development of the major monuments of Greek art and architecture from the 8th century to the mid-5th century B.C.

title: Later Greek Art & Architecture
description: Course Description: Study of the art and architecture of later Classical and Hellenistic Greece, from the mid-5th century to the 1st century B.C.

title: Roman Art & Architecture
description: Course Description: Art and architecture of Rome and the Roman Empire, from the founding of Rome through the 4th century C.E.

title: Greek Religion & Society
description: Course Description: Cults, festivals, and rituals of Greek religious practice and their relationship to Greek social and political institutions, and to Greek private life. Includes discussion of major sanctuaries at Olympia, Delphi, Athens, and others.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): A lower division Classics (CLA) course or consent of instructor.

title: Architecture & Urbanism in Mediterranean Antiquity
description: Course Description: Architecture and urban development in the ancient Near East, Greece, and Rome. Special emphasis on the social structure of the ancient city as expressed in its architecture, and on the interaction between local traditions and the impact of Greco-Roman urbanism.

title: Roman Religions
description: Course Description: Roman religion from republic to empire. Gods, rituals, and festivals at Rome; sacrifice, sacred places, magic. Gender roles, social status, national identity. Influences from other cultures, especially Egypt and the eastern Mediterranean.

title: Ancient Mediterranean Technologies
description: Course Description: Practical recreation of and experimentation with ancient technologies. Projects may include pottery and glazing, papyrus and ink making, textile arts, woodworking, pneumatic devices, automata, medicinal processes.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): A lower division Classics (CLA) course or consent of instructor.

title: Senior Seminar
description: Course Description: Advanced interdisciplinary study of a problem in the ancient Mediterranean world using the techniques of history, archaeology, art history and philology.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Completion of one upper division course in Latin (LAT), Greek (GRK) or Hebrew (HEB) or consent of instructor.

title: Special Study for Honors Students
description: Course Description: Directed reading, research and writing culminating in the completion of a senior honors thesis under the direction of faculty advisor.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Admission to the Honors Program; consent of faculty member supervising honors thesis.

title: Special Study for Honors Students
description: Course Description: Directed reading, research, and writing culminating in the completion of a senior honors thesis under the direction of faculty advisor.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Admission to the Honors Program and consent of faculty member supervising honors thesis.

title: Community Tutoring in Classical Languages
description: Course Description: Supervised instruction of Greek or Latin in nearby schools by qualified students in department.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Consent of instructor.

title: Directed Group Study
description: Course Description: Directed group study.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Consent of instructor; upper division standing.

title: Special Study for Advanced Undergraduates
description: Course Description: Special study for advanced undergraduates.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Consent of instructor; upper division standing.

title: Approaches to the Classical Past
description: Course Description: Survey of major areas of classical scholarship, with special emphasis on the continuing impact of Mediterranean antiquity on later literature, history, art, and culture.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Graduate student status or consent of instructor.

title: Approaches to the Classics Past
description: Course Description: Research project on major area of Classical scholarship, with special emphasis on the continuing impact of Mediterranean antiquity on later literature, history, art, and culture.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): CLA 200A; graduate student status or consent of instructor.

title: Introduction to Classical Philology
description: Course Description: Survey of major contemporary areas of classical scholarship with special attention devoted to current problems in literary and textual criticism.

title: Homer
description: Course Description: Readings in the Iliad and Odyssey: the origins and transmission of the poems.

title: Vergil
description: Course Description: Reading of selected books of the Bucolics, Georgics, and Aeneid. Emphasis will be placed on the study of Vergilean poetic language.

title: Greek & Roman Comedy
description: Course Description: Historical and critical problems in Aristophanes or New Comedy.

title: Latin Lyric & Elegy
description: Course Description: Critical examination of the works of Catullus, Horace, or Propertius.

title: Greek Historiography
description: Course Description: Development of historical writing in Greece.

title: Greek Drama
description: Course Description: Literary and philological analysis of the plays of Euripides, Sophocles, or Aeschylus.

title: Research
description: Course Description: Research.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Consent of instructor.

title: Teaching Assistant Training Practicum
description: Course Description: Teaching assistant training practicum.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Graduate standing.

title: Introduction to Translational Research
description: Course Description: Introduction to the CRGG program and overview of major clinical research topics. Overview of basic clinical skills needed to accomplish CRGG mentored research project.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Consent of instructor; one of the following degrees: MD, DDS, DMD, OD, ND, DO, PharmD, DVM, PhD or DNS in nursing; application and acceptance into the Clinical Research Graduate Group, K30 program or other SOM/CTSC training programs.

title: Strategies for Grant Writing
description: Course Description: Practical skills and strategies to create successful grant proposals in NIH style/format. Generating ideas, identifying and accessing research resources, grant components, specific aims, background and significance, preliminary studies, budgets, and bios. Matriculation through UC system, and resubmissions. (Former MDS 461CR.)
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Consent of instructor; completed one of the following degrees: MD, DDS, DMD, OD, ND, DO, PharmD, DVM, PhD or DNS in nursing. Application and acceptance into the Clinical Research Graduate Group, K30 program or other SOM/CTSC training program.

title: Introduction to Clinical Research
description: Course Description: Anatomy and physiology of conducting clinical epidemiologic research. Familiarity with three basic study designs: cross-sectional, case-control, and cohort. Discussion of principles of measurements in clinical epidemiological studies, basic methods for analyzing data, and ethical issues involved in conducting research.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Consent of instructor; completed one of the following degrees: MD, DDS, DMD, OD, ND, DO, PharmD, DVM, PhD or DNS in nursing; application and acceptance into the Clinical Research Graduate Group, MRCTP program, Clinical Research Certificate or other SOM/CTSC training programs.

title: Methods in Clinical Research
description: Course Description: Overview of major approaches to clinical research, including health services research techniques, informatics, GCRC, and preclinical methodologies to enhance clinical projects. Overview of UC Davis clinical research support infrastructure. Methodologies applicable to clinical research and its multi-disciplinary perspective.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Consent of instructor; Completed one of the following degrees: MD, DDS, DMD, OD, ND, DO, PharmD, DVM, PhD or DNS in nursing; application and acceptance into the Clinical Research Graduate Group, TL1/T32, KL2/K12, MCRTP, or other SOM training program.

title: The Ethics of Research
description: Course Description: Ethical responsibilities, major questions in ethics, the role of trust in scientific research, and the application of ethical principles, concepts, and values.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Consent of instructor.

title: Team Science
description: Course Description: Today’s scientific challenges necessitate cross-disciplinary engagement and high collaboration levels. Offers guidance on how best to engage in team science to pursue complex questions, work effectively with team members, and produce high impact research that meets society’s needs.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Participation in CTSC Research Education and Training Programs, or consent of instructor.

title: Introduction to Grant Writing, I
description: Course Description: First in a two-quarter series. Scholars are encouraged to enroll in both classes. The two-course sequence provides training in practical aspects of competitive grant writing. The focus is NIH, but information will apply to other funding agencies.

title: Introduction to Grant Writing, II
description: Course Description: Second in a two-quarter series. Two-course sequence provides training in practical aspects of competitive grant writing.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): CLH 208; consent of instructor.

title: Principles & Methods of Comparative Effectiveness Research
description: Course Description: Provides an introduction to Comparative Effectiveness Research (CER) and methods for conducting CER.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Consent of instructor; familiarity with research methodology; course in introductory statistics.

title: Critical Assessment of the Biomedical Literature
description: Course Description: Exposure to topical issues and controversies in the design of interdisciplinary translational research, with an emphasis on critical assessment of the biomedical and health sciences literature. Extends students’ knowledge of study design through practical application.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Consent of instructor.

title: Introduction to Stem Cell Biology
description: Course Description: Introduction to Stem Cell Biology. Each week will focus on different aspects of stem cells, including general concepts, stem cells in lower organisms, embryonic stem cells and cellular reprogramming.

title: Biodesign I
description: Course Description: Focuses on the principles of needs identification and invention of biomedical technologies. Two part course provides a basic understanding of the elements of the innovation process and how to translate these principles into biomedical device design.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Consent of instructor.

title: Biodesign II
description: Course Description: Focuses on the implementation of biomedical technologies and translational process. Two part course provides a basic understanding of the elements of the innovation process and how to translate these principles into biomedical device design.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): CLH 214A; consent of instructor.

title: Clinical Trials in Medicine
description: Course Description: Importance, design, and execution of clinical trials.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): CLH 244 C or better; SPH 205AY C or better; or equivalent; or consent of instructor.

title: Healthcare Delivery Science – Implementing, Improving, & Disseminating Health Innovations
description: Course Description: Methods for moving evidence-based interventions into real-world settings. Strategies for communicating information on evidence-based interventions to health practitioners, improvements in healthcare delivery, and techniques to promote the systematic uptake of evidence-based practices into routine care to improve health outcomes.

title: Basics of Stem & Progenitor Cells
description: Course Description: Lecture designed for graduate students who have experience in cell culture techniques. Designed to give a broad overview of the field and current cells of interest to the greater research community.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): MCP 200L; MCP 200; consent of instructor; graduate standing.

title: Ethical Issues in Stem Cell Biology
description: Course Description: Critical presentation and analysis of recent articles in stem cell biology and small group discussions of the ethical issues surrounding this area of research.
prerequisites: Prerequisite(s): Consent of instructor; gaduate standing.

title: Stem Cell Biology & current Good Manufacturing Practice
